If you once learned how to tell time by the migration, you have forgotten. It isn’t a forgetting that stings. It is the generous sort that trusts the sky to remember you.

You learned to ride a bicycle late. The street gently sloped toward a neighbor’s forsythia, an amber fountain assembled from a thousand delicate suns. The first time you stopped falling, your knees felt like loose bolts. The air smelled like tar and lilac. Someone cheered too loudly from a porch and you hated how good it felt. You tasted iron where you bit your lip and thought, so this is what blood is for: a red autograph on the world to certify that you were here, moving, briefly balanced on a question. Brakes squealed; the chain clicked; you became one more mechanism, a child-sized engine turning the spring into a choir of blackbirds fleeing the hedge. The body is a machine that likes to believe in love. The machine works best when it does.

There was a storm once that pressed the town flat. The kind of storm that rearranges names. You recognized the end of it not by the quiet—storms teach you a new kind of quiet—but by the sound of people locking and unlocking things again, testing what still answered. The square lost power, and the locksmith’s shop went soft and dark. People drifted in on the damp smell of the street, and he lit a small candle warped from years beside the register. Watch hands lay slack at their posts; a velvet pad threw back its shine. Someone laughed in the foolish, relieved way we laugh when it turns out the end of the world is only a blackout. He told a story then, not to pass the time but to touch it. About a lighthouse whose keeper wrote letters to the ships he couldn’t see, every night, in longhand. Dear Vessel of Unknown Name, he would begin, if you can read this, then ink can fly. He tucked their unwritten replies in a tin. Storms came. Men came. Wars arrived. The tin went with him into the ground. What remained was the habit of attention, which is also a key.

When power returned, it did so in a shy shrug. Fluorescents stuttered awake; the bulletin board rediscovered its color. People found reasons to stay a little longer anyway. A boy spun a tin tag on the counter and called it a comet, a coin, a planet with a history hard to fold into a pocket. The locksmith wound the old clocks, one by one. Their ticks did not agree, but they harmonized, like a field of crickets whose quarrel is their chorus. Outside, puddles grew eyes and streets remembered how to glitter. You could feel the town relatch around you, a ship’s hatch spun snug against weather. Even the trees had that particular exhausted posture that means we lived, we kept standing.

At home, the bowl was a nebula of metal and lint. You turned the key of your own door because belonging is a practice more than a right. Later, over dishes, you rehearsed the inventory of your day the way you inventory small losses because each tells you what you meant to keep: a name almost remembered, a headline that dulled the knife and so was not read; a single dandelion mowing down the lawn’s idea of itself. Somewhere a train processioned across the night, its horn unwinding the town thread by careful thread so it could lace it again. You thought of doors that never learned the word no. You thought of the birds whose map is a pull in the blood, their keys internal, weightless, shining only to themselves.

If you go out, lock the door softly. If you return late, let your shoes invent a way to be quiet. Leave a spare beneath the honest stone that looks like a turtle. Remember the neighbor who knocks not because the package is yours but because the knock is. When the kettle breathes, answer in kind. If you misplace what lets you in, wait on the stoop with your empty hands and your name. Someone will pass with a story that fits. Someone will say, try this. Someone will show you the small hinge in the universe that opens without any metal at all, and it will feel like hunger, and it will feel like grace.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
On the nights when fog sits low, the radio fills with a kind of animal breathing. Static grows fur along every syllable. You can lean toward it, cup your hands like you might around a candle, and feel the heat of other lives carry through. I keep a little logbook near the transmitter, the lined kind with feint blue veins and a place for time, wind, and remarks. Remarks are where the truth lives.

It’s an old lighthouse. The stairs complain in a voice that’s part rust, part song. The prisms of the lens wear a sleeve of salt, and though I clean them twice a day there’s always a lace of the ocean left clinging. The light turns, unhurried, a steady arc laid over water like the arm of someone promising to stay. Sometimes the promise is enough. Sometimes it isn’t.

The fox comes on Wednesdays. I don’t know how it tells time—maybe by the routines that stack themselves up here like bowls—but there it is at the door when the kitchen smells of onions. It looks at me with the polite exasperation of a neighbor who knows I am too soft to say no. I tear a piece of bread and the fox takes it gently, as if I am a fawn-hearted thing that could shatter. On the weeks when the fog refuses to lift, that small transaction feels like proof that language is not all we have.

I learned the names of stars from my sister on the roof of the house where we didn’t live for long. She would tip her head back until her hair made a river and say, there’s Deneb, see? Vega’s the bright one, Altair’s over there; they make a triangle but also a bird. It was important to her that I notice how the same points could be called different things depending on the map. We put plates over our hearts like armor and held sticks like oars and promised that someday we’d find a sea of light. She kept her promises better than I kept mine.

The logbook I use belonged to two keepers before me. Their handwriting leans different directions. One used to underline wind: fresh breeze, then stiff breeze underlined twice. Another drew tiny clouds with little stitches of rain falling from their bellies. Between entries they left small surprises: a pressed leaf, a faded receipt for kerosene, one perfect cartoon of a gull wearing boots. I add my own offerings—stripes of radio chatter, a red ring around the night we lost power, a note about the whale that slept under a skin of moonlight and sounded like a door closing gently.

What comes through on the radio is a census of the immediate world. Fishermen trading recipes with the same exactness they use for coordinates. A woman who radios every year on her father’s birthday to say, I remember, I remember, I remember, and then there’s only weather. Once a pilot whose voice shook like glass until I said, say what you can see, and he did—white shingles, a windbreak of pines, the hillside like a shoulder—and in the saying found his way.

I keep a box of index cards on the shelf with the cups. Each card carries a small thing I want to return to: the light of a ferry swallowing a whole school of moths, phosphorescence on a broken wave like teeth in the dark, thirteen pelicans low as punctuation. On nights when the sea sounds like it’s chewing its own name I shuffle these cards and try to remember that I have been alive in ways that did not ache.

Some days the mainland feels like a rumor I half believe. They say most ships don’t need lights anymore, not really. There are satellites and systems and digital charts that update while you are blinking. But I have watched a captain make a long slow turn toward a beam of old light because every other screen had gone strange with rain or with error, and that beam said, I am here, the world is still particular, your hull still holds.

The storm that made a myth of itself began as a warm edge to the wind. I wrote the numbers: barometer falling, waves building, remarks: gulls absurdly cheerful, fox absent. When it hit, it hit all at once, the way people leave when they’re done promising they won’t. The generator coughed awake and I went room to room like an usher, checking the seals, the lantern fuel, the little graveyard of fuses we keep for luck. The tower flexed like a rib cage. That I am always astonished by how everything moves is, I think, how I stay.

Around midnight a voice smudged itself across the band. It was young and trying very hard not to be. We took a wave wrong, the voice said. The jib’s a mess. The engine—okay, the engine is not okay. I asked for position and got a string of numbers with a wobble in it, like a thread pulled through thick cloth. Say what you can see, I said, and there was a long sleeve of rain between us, but then: a little island like two fists, some white water, a blinking light that could be a boat or could be a trick. I told them the rock’s name and didn’t tell them that the name means the place where you learn how sharp something is.

I spoke until my throat felt like old rope. I said hold what you can, I said keep the bow to it, I said you don’t have to be brave, just busy. The light made its steady turn. Once a flash caught the rain at the right angle and the whole world was needles. In the log I wrote: voices cut out, then back in; hummingbird heart in my hand. I don’t know why I wrote that except I do.

They came around the fist of rock like people changing their minds. The hull juddered; the radio filled with a sound like applause, unearned but welcome. Later, a small voice in the bleed of static said thank you, and I wrote that too. Remarks: gratitude, unplaceable. Wind: ridiculous.

Morning is a different country from night and I am a poor citizen of both. The storm left a grammar on the beach: commas of driftwood, a question mark made of kelp, a whole paragraph of bladderwrack shrilling under the touch of sun. The fox returned and we made our small treaty again. I boiled coffee so black it could extinguish itself. The light rested. It always rests as if it never had to work, and I forgive it because I know better.

In the town they have a market that smells like peaches even in months when peaches are a kind of fiction. The clerk tells me news as if I have been living underwater, which in a way I have. There’s always someone building something, someone leaving, someone making a fuss about the big clock that refuses to keep good time. I buy more onions than I need and a cheap deck of cards because I like the promise that something can be shuffled until it is fair.

Sometimes, when the frequency is clear, I talk low into the radio to no one in particular. I say the names of things: reef, shoal, bay, drift, scud, wrack, spindrift, fetch. I say lighthouse like a person would say home and mean it in the old sense, as in where you place your tools when you are finished and the tools remember you. I like to think the words go out and stick to the air, and when someone needs them they find their hands landing in the right place.

It would be cleaner storytelling if I said that every night has a climax, that every trouble finds land. The truth is softer and more bearable: most nights nothing much happens and I write it down anyway. Wind: tired. Sea: agreeable. Remarks: fox certain, stars late. There’s a tenderness in accounting for the unremarkable. It’s the kind of tenderness that, once you learn it, you start seeing everywhere.

If anyone asks what the job is, I say it’s keeping. Not rescuing, not warning, not shining—though those happen too—but keeping: a light, a list, a promise, a habit, a name. The map I carry is not the kind you fold but the kind that lives behind the eyes, that bright braid of where the cries came from and where they went. When I’m old, if I am lucky enough to be old, I will sit somewhere with a soft chair and a window and shuffle my cards and say, once, in the fog, someone said I remember three times like a bell. And if no one is there to hear it, the air will keep it. The air keeps almost everything, if you ask it gently.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
A shoreline is a place where maps apologize. The ink comes right up to the surf and then lifts its hands. Everything steady becomes almost, the way a chair is when someone just stood from it, the ghost of a sitting still warm in the wood. I grew up inland, with lake horizons that faked ocean, and so the first time I saw the tide pull a line of wrack backwards I believed, briefly, that the world had learned to breathe.

There’s a lighthouse keeper in my head who trims wicks that don’t exist. He moves in circles, polishing lenses, setting the pendulum of a clock that always loses a few honest seconds to the wind. He knows that light is only useful if the weather decides to allow it. He carries a thermos of coffee that tastes like burnt hope and keeps a notebook of ships that never asked to be saved.

In the city, morning collects in low places. The gutter holds a river of leaf-brown tea. A delivery truck makes a sound like backing into a mistake. Someone slips a croissant into a paper bag and the paper breathes. Cyclists arc around parked cars as if they’ve been told a secret about angles and mercy. A small dog wears a sweater knitted by somebody’s grandmother’s hands, those hands that now probably smell like orange peels, lotion, and coin.

My mother could whistle into a bottle in a way that made the kitchen sound haunted. She called it borrowing the room from the room. Wind in a glass, breath in a mouth, notes that do not know where they’re going but go anyway. When she was tired she would hum the same three notes over and over, the simplest lighthouse. I think that’s what love looks like from far away: a repeated pattern you’re grateful for even when you’ve stopped naming it.

If memory has a unit, it’s neither minute nor photograph. It’s the small hitch you hear when a friend says it’s fine and you know it isn’t. It’s the way your fingers remember the soft divot on the seventh stair where everyone in your family put their weight. It’s the light ring left by a glass on a table that woodenly forgives you. Memory is what you can recite with your eyes closed, that prayer you learned when you needed to pretend to be holy.

Another unit of measurement: the world by its quiets. The quiet between two people at a bus stop who both know they will never see each other again. The quiet inside a museum when the security guard coughs in the wing of ancient pottery. The quiet where a spider remakes what somebody else walked through. The quiet in an elevator where a child reads each button like a constellation.

I keep wanting to write an instruction manual for being lost. Step one: notice what still knows where it is. The coffee shop that smells exactly like a Tuesday in late March. The plant that leans toward any light without apology. The old man reading the newspaper who sighs like a kettle on low. Step two: carry a safety pin, a bandage, and one sentence you believe enough to set down under your foot. Step three: when you ask for directions, admit that you are not just looking for a street.

In the library, I once found a note in a book that said: This page always makes me feel less alone. The page was about a woman learning to peel an orange without breaking the peel. I didn’t cry, but something inside me arranged itself as if it had been crying and now it could stop. There should be a word for that. There should be a word for opening a drawer and finding a smell of winter.

If you could only keep five smells: rain on hot asphalt, cut grass on your calves, bread cracking open, the sweat of a person you love after they have run to meet you, and your own shoulder in a sweater after you have been somewhere worth being. I suppose pine, tomatoes on the vine, the salt-metal of the sea might argue their case. I suppose the ghosts of all unlucky onions would say, Hey now.

I write letters I do not send. Dear Autumn, you are very good at orange and I admire that about you, but sometimes you mistake my face for a field. Dear First Mosquito of Summer, I know it isn’t personal, but my blood is so very narrow in my veins; be kind. Dear Person Three Seats Up on the Bus Who Dropped a Photograph, you should know the boy in the picture has a smile that looks like a kite finally getting air. I tucked it into the space between the seats so it could wait for you like a faithful dog.

Sometimes I want to be a bench. Just a place that holds what it is asked to hold until the question changes. A bench doesn’t mind the rain. It knows it cannot go inside. It knows that eventually the sun will warm the slats again and there will be gum under it and initials in it and a light dust of pollen making everything yellow and holy.

At the museum near closing, the paintings yawn. You can’t hear it unless you let your eyes unfocus slightly and admit how good they are at pretending to be windows. The guards exchange gossip in hand signals. The marble thinks about glaciers. Somewhere, a child touches a velvet rope and feels like a heist.

I have pressed my ear to a seashell and heard not the ocean but my own waiting. I have put a glass over a wasp, slid a postcard underneath, and carried the furious, beautiful thing outside, where it took off as if it had never been inside anything. I have pretended to know the names of stars and then been forgiven by the night for lying.

There are days when my mind is a kitchen after a party. There are days when it is a meadow / a guitar case / a sink full of sky. On the good ones, I make a soup called Staying. Ingredients: whatever you have, cut into bite-sized pieces. Salt enough to taste like what you’ve been through. Heat until everything is softer than it was. Share without apologizing for the recipe.

What do we do with the spare minutes? The ones you get when the train is four minutes late, when the kettle takes a breath before it boils, when the meeting could have been an email and wasn’t. You could answer a text with a real question. You could look at your hands and say, Thank you, you odd soft tools. You could learn the name of a bird not because names matter to the bird but because saying them makes your mouth a better room to live in.

A friend told me the color of almost is that moment right before the streetlights turn on in summer. You can see everything and nothing is illuminated. I think almost is a virtue. I think we treat arriving like a trophy when all the trophies I know gather dust and all the almosts I know teach me to adjust my eyes.

A shoreline is a place where we practice not owning anything. The water keeps what it wants. The sand keeps what can’t escape it. We keep stepping forward and the earth steps back. Somewhere a lighthouse keeper is winding a clock that will betray him in the smallest, truest way. Somewhere, a map is being drawn in pencil so that it can be corrected when the tide proves the cartographer human.

Tonight I will go to sleep listening for the soft click of the world correcting itself. If I cannot hear it, I will pretend a radio is scanning the sky, finding the music of distant storms, the static of half-heard advice, the heartbeat someone left on a voicemail by accident. I will think of a bench holding a coat and the shape of someone inside it. I will think of an orange peel coming off in one long animal skin. I will think of a word that means the relief after not crying, and I will not know it, and I will be fine.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
On the third floor of a building with no address, there is an office that receives every letter never sent. It exists wherever a thought pauses at the rim of a mouth and retreats, wherever a hand hovers over a mailbox and turns away. The hinge that keeps it there is simple stubbornness and complicated mercy. Inside, the light is patient, sifting through high windows and resting on stacks of envelopes sorted by the temperature of their hesitation. Some are warm with urgency that cooled to ash. Some are cold as bridges in winter. The dust is polite. The air smells faintly of stamps, even though none have ever been licked. I know this because I work there, though no one hired me, and I have no paycheck except time.

Each morning I unlock the cabinets with keys that do not jingle. The labels are not names but angles: Toward Courage, Away From Harm, Under The Weather, Over One's Head. Within, the unsent accumulate like snowdrifts that never melt. I do not read them all. That would be a theft. I brush my fingers along the edges, listening to the different paper speak. Coarse apologies rasp. Smooth stationery whispers like lake water. Newsprint clucks. On the days when storms gather in other people's throats, we feel it here, a low pressure of might-have-beens. The windows ache the way old scars do before rain. My work is simple: I measure, I alphabetize by heartbeat, I file by the weight of regret.

Sometimes an envelope starts to hum. It means the person who wrote it is walking past a postbox and feeling the gravity of nearly. It means the thought has warmed again, a coal breathed upon. I have learned not to interfere. The rule, written nowhere, is that I cannot send what could have been sent, because history is a tangled hair and I only own a comb. But sometimes I set the humming envelope on the windowsill, and the light does something to it, a soft unraveling, a suggestion of road. A few have vanished this way, which I do not take personally. When they go, the room gains an ounce of brightness, and somewhere, perhaps, a shoulder releases.

There are categories so specific they make me smile. Letters written in bathrooms at weddings. Letters drafted upon learning a favorite tree was cut down. Letters to teachers twenty years later to say the grain of praise still lodges beneath the tongue. There is a drawer for poems addressed to rain that were never brave enough to be called poems. There is a metal box for the monster questions: Will you forgive me, Could I have this back, Do you love me. Sometimes the boxes rattle. We tighten their lids. We do not read. It is not piety. It is a respect for the architecture of decision, which is sometimes elegant and sometimes merely a door that will not open.

Once, a letter arrived with my name on it. The handwriting was my own, but steadier. It had the brittle quiet of paper that crossed a winter. I kept it unopened for a week, tucked under the scale we use to weigh the rumors. I made tea and set the cup beside it each morning, as if warmth could teach it to behave. On the eighth day I slit the envelope. Inside was a single sentence, written twice. It said: Stop concluding for everyone else. Stop concluding for everyone else. The second time it was underlined. I folded it back into its envelope, then into another, then another, like a child making a worry charm, small as a seed.

We keep a bowl of stamps no country recognizes. People who find us by accident sometimes ask for one as a souvenir. I let them choose, though I warn that they stick to nothing outside this room. A boy once pressed a blue sun to his forehead and said it felt like a doorbell. An old woman sorted through them by taste, touching each to her tongue like the ghosts of strange fruit. Another visitor looked around and said, This is just a room full of cowardice. He was right, but only the way a weather report is right: there will be rain, and also people singing under umbrellas, and also gutters learning how to speak.

The building has no address, but deliveries arrive all the same. We receive crates of unsent postcards, their pictures bleaching into an equal sky: the same beach, the same mountain, the same cathedral blessing the same pigeons. A child somewhere writes, Wish you were here, then erases you and writes anyone. A man writes nothing at all and slides the blank card between bills, a galleon among ledgers, then loses his nerve and keeps it. I stack their unsaid cities into skyline after skyline. When the afternoon is quiet, I imagine the addresses would have learned one another, a correspondence of destinations talking among themselves: Do you ever tire of being longed for.

At closing I sweep. Paper flits like pale moths and comes to rest again. I turn the lamp off from the cord because the switch has grown superstitious. I count the steps to the stairwell: thirteen, then the one I always forget. On the landing there is a window that frames a sliver of lake. I stand there longer than necessary and practice not deciding. The water knows how to hold every unmade ripple at once. It turns the sky into something it can keep. When I finally walk down, the city feels like an envelope facing its addressee, the whole night gathered under a name, the stamp of the moon damp on its corner, waiting, waiting, then held.

In the morning it will begin again: breath pausing, ink stammering, hands hovering above a mouth, a mailbox, a beating door. I will unlock what never opened, sort the weather, listen for the hum, and promise to send nothing, except maybe myself, someday, soon.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
I map the city by smells, because they’ve never lied to me. You can barrel down a street and believe you know everything by how it looks—the angle of sunlight on brick, the new restaurants, the scaffolding with its shroud—but vision flatters and edits. Smell is less diplomatic. It arrives without preface, it refuses to curate, and when it drops you somewhere you’ve been before, it is the whole of you who goes.

On my desk there’s a map that looks like weather. I draw faint lines where the scents gather, as if they’re pressure systems traveling over roofs and under bridges. This is the lilac front; it blooms hard for three weeks in May and drowns out doorways and bus exhalations and the hot brake smell of delivery trucks. This is the after-rain zone, the old iron and penny taste that rises from grates in the hour after a summer storm. On the brittle edge of the map, circled in blue pencil, is the curious plume from the pier where the fishmongers hose the day back into the river. It has the salt of work in it. It is better in the morning.

People come to see me with things they want to find again. They bring wallets, old watches, tarnished teaspoons, the cuff of a sweater. They hold them like contraband. They say it the same way: I know this is strange, but. They are really asking for a time. For someone. A man sits on my low chair and turns his hat over and over. He wants the smell of his grandfather’s wool coat after he came inside from shoveling. Another wants the sharp, medicinal sweetness of the theater foyer in 1988, the one with the carpet like a migraine, because that’s where she sat through bad movies with a person who is gone and she has a cough now and she wants to sit there again. There is a woman who returns every spring, who wants the exact smell of sunscreen and plastic pool and lilac from a specific June when her son was seven and quiet and had not yet learned to be cruel to himself. She brings a page torn from a magazine he folded then, as if the paper had learned it.

I tell them directly what I can and cannot do. Smell is stubborn in the wrong ways. There is no guarantee, only pursuit. But pursuit counts. Even when we fail, people leave lighter for having handed the quest to someone whose hands don’t shake. I am careful with their hopes. Hope, too, has a scent: a string of aldehydes like lemons two rooms away.

My tools are time and weather and a pair of shoes I can walk for hours in. People assume I carry beans to reset the nose, but rinsing with coffee is mostly theater for the sales floor. I use my own clean skin, the unstated smell of the inside of my elbow. I don’t smoke and I don’t wear perfume, though I do allow myself the rose soap my grandmother used; it was the only thing in her life she wasted at all. Half the work is patience. Scents arc and fall like birds between morning and late afternoon, and what is pungent at noon goes mute by dusk. I have learned the way laundry moves on wind when it hangs high over courtyards, I have learned that bookstores smell different on Thursdays. I have learned to knock on loading bay doors and ask the day’s menu of motor oil.

Once, a boy came in and asked me to find the smell of snow. He was twelve, his wrists all hinge and bone, his eyes soft with the plea of someone who longs for a thing not yet lived. He had seen snow in videos and wanted to know it before winter came, to know if it would be like biting foil. He was too young for longing that travels ahead of you. I brought him to the skating rink when they cut the ice. The shavings blew up and fell like a second, artificial weather, and he inhaled with his whole small chest and said oh. It wasn’t snow but it was in the neighborhood, metallic and cold and full of the physics of surface and cut. He grinned. I put a dot on the map: rink chill, not winter.

Another time, I stood on a corner with a woman whose hair was going and who wanted to smell a very particular school hallway at exactly five minutes after five p.m. in 1969. She had been a secretary and she liked to stay late. There was wax and old paper and warm mouse fur—if you have never sniffed a broad-backed book in July, you don’t know that smell. There was something else I could not place, some resinous sweetness. We tried eight different buildings with the same aged wooden floors. We tried the old hospital’s administrative wing, the back corridor of the church. Finally, at a community center, in the basement where the city keeps chairs that smell like varnish and gym rope, she inhaled and took my hand as if bracing against a wind. “Here,” she said. “It’s the erasers.” We found the chalk board and the felt erasers sleeping on it, and there was the faint ghost of calcium and lanolin. Quietly, like conspirators, we clapped them twice. Dust rose like elderly applause. We went outside and cried in the sun for a little while.

I did not become this by design. I lost a thing and have not found it. It is the smell of a particular autumn after the house fire that did not take the house, the particular damp wool of the blanket we slept under after, that had not dried properly because we couldn’t mind the dryer noise with fans going all night to keep the smell of smoke moving through. There was the ghost of leaves crushed underfoot and someone’s pipe two doors down. There was chimney smoke that wasn’t ours. It was the first year we were together, and your hands were ash-gray from scrubbing, and we ate toasted things until we were tired of brown, and everything else in the world tasted scorched but you did not. I have paired combinations that sidle up to it, I have found its body double in the warming room of a bakery where the loaves proof in woolen blankets, I have sniffed a hundred hearths and wept quietly in stairwells so no one would have to wonder if they should call anyone. Close, close, but not the exact. This is not punishment. It is a way to continue.

I go out early, before the day learns its lines. Dawn has a kindly mildew in the old neighborhoods, and the dumpsters at the florist’s smell like weddings. There is a bakery that fries dough on Thursdays and the oil smell lingers on the curve of the freeway ramp; it is a bready wake for commuters. There is a garage where the mechanic leaves orange peels on the windowsill and the scent punches the grease in the throat, breaks it into something you can stand in. On hot days the city smells like pennies and possibility. In winter, if you stand under the great vent of the museum late at night, you can catch a story of wool coats, school buses, and the dry sleep of canvases. Someone who has seen me there wrote me a note that said I worried you were a ghost. I wrote back: most of us are. Later, I crossed out the line. It smelled too neat.

If I have faith in anything it is the way the air keeps a small archive for us. The world disposes and recycles relentlessly, but it leaks evidence. Smell is evidence in the shape of surprise. People think it dwells in objects, and sometimes it does, but more often it lives in rooms made of time. That is why it can’t be shipped. You must arrive in it.

One night, deep summer, a thunderstorm walked up Main like a parade and the power went. The street turned black. The fans in a dozen restaurants died and the kitchens exhaled what they had been making—the garlic and wine and browned butter and lemon, the preserved lemon of a cook who had used it like a vow. A bus slowed and a woman laughed on a balcony. Then the rain came hard and beat the heat out of the stones, and the city exhaled its mineral self. I stood under the edge of the awning and breathed like a thief.

For a while this is enough. I walk the lanes and alleys and write the air. I ferry people to what they can still touch. I can name at least nine different vanillas: bakery cream, candle wax, body lotion, marshmallow, fondant, bourbon bean, dry pod, soda fountain syrup, and the dusted envelope of old letters. There are vanillas I have not met yet. There is the exact wool-and-smoke of that not-quite fire, hovering in a corner of a night I haven’t reached.

On the map there is a place I have not marked. It moves with me. Some mornings it is at the intersection where the bus brakes sing. Some nights it is curled in the stairwell outside my door. It is the smell of my own life, turning. I am learning to notice it. I am learning not to flee.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
On the city ledger I am an olfactory surveyor, grade II, a title that feels like a suit two sizes too big. The kids in the alleys call me the nose-cartographer, which is worse but true. I map what hangs in the air after a place is already mostly gone.

My kit is simple: glass vials, beeswax seals, a hand pump with bellows like a sparrow’s lung. A notebook for layers and notes. A flat shard of copper I use to warm resins. A jar of roasted coffee beans to clear the palette, a habit I stole from perfumers and coffee shops alike. Salt, for drawing damp. A lighter, for coaxing caramel into speaking.

It is easier at dawn. Smell is ancient and tireless, but it wears the day like a sweater—diesel and sweat and printer toner snuggling into everything. At sunrise, the night has brushed some of it off. That is when I take most commissions.

This morning the job is the São Vicente Bakery, sixty-seven years of bread. The ovens went out two months ago. The grandson and developers took the bricks and the stories and left a white scar where the ovens kissed the room. The city has asked me to make an olfactory record for the Heritage Lattice that floats over sidewalks and old streets. Clipboard language. The widow, Dona Lídia, asked me for something smaller: I want the warm cliff when the bread is pulled. I want my husband’s palms without opening my chest.

She is waiting on a milk crate when I arrive, palms like folded crust. She points to the proofing room. The real smell isn’t the oven, she says. It’s in there. It’s damp and promise. It was always damp, and sometimes it made the paper on the racks go wavy. We used to line the pans with fish wrappers in the seventies when paper was paper, you know? Newsprint and ink, and the sweet stuck to the headlines. You’d think sugar and anchovy would be enemies, but they know each other’s names.

I write “ink” and “damp bread wall” and “proofing as tide” in my notebook. I kneel by the proofing room’s threshold. There is a geometry to smell that isn’t Euclid’s. Corners keep secrets. Heat loosens old varnish into speech. I warm the copper with the lighter, hold it near the weighted door, and a whisper comes loose: flour sift, low yeast hum, a old wool coat that hung beside the door every winter and remembered all winters. The neon sign’s ozone rasp is in the grout, a fishbone note. I pass the bellows gently—one, two, three—and latch the first vial with a beeswax kiss.

We collect in layers the way people argue about decades. Dona Lídia says the seventies smelled like oranges because for a while, they bought crates of green ones and left them on the flour sacks to ripen in the warmth, a florist’s trick. The eighties smelled like cheap deodorant and hair spray and the almonds from the wedding cakes that her son turned into little beige fortunes. I write benzaldehyde, which is the science word the almond hides behind, but in the notebook it becomes “first ring slipped on a finger.”

I have done this long enough to know that a place never speaks in just one voice. Lost tram lines say hot copper and brake dust. There is a church whose floorboards hold the pepper ghosts of candle smoke and a thousand sweaters. Under the old library’s staircase, I found pockets of book glue that crackled like old paper when warmed, and a winter’s worth of woolen mittens dried there that had left behind their own lanolin sigh. The Ethics Board says we do not extract from private dwellings without consent, as if consent could be a signature penned in air. I leave offerings, a superstition my boss calls waste: I leave salt under sills. I leave a fragment of cedar. A thread.

I set a paper cone in the center of the bakery floor and pile in two gray pebbles that look like nothing. They are a pair of porphyrized sugar lumps from a fairground, scavenged last year when the Ferris wheel’s generator coughed its last and the air broke into cola and machine sweat. Old sugar is a helpful liar: you warm it and it tells you about every caramel it has ever been near. On the copper, the pebble softens, and a ribbon of butterscotch and something faint—cardboard?—leaks up. Dona Lídia shakes her head. No. It was warm and damp, but not sweet-sweet. It had a little sting. Like the first day of winter in your nose.

I scrawl “sting.” The sting might be acetic acid from fermenting dough. It might be the ghost of vinegar used to wipe the benches. It might be the river fog that padded in on mornings when the tides were right, weaving into the flour dust and making a weather that never touched the forecast.

There is a boy under the counter, cross-legged in the light like a seed in a glass of water. He has dusted his jeans with flour and drawn a map on the tile with a stick of cinnamon, a child’s coastline of bays and teeth. Whatcha doing? I ask. Cape Cinnamon, he says, pointing to a horned promontory. My aunt said the bakery smelled strongest when the green banana boxes came because the truck always got there early and hid in the fog. My uncle hated fog. Said it made the flour clot. The bananas were for the corner store but they set them here so no one would steal them and the whole place would smell like... He waves his hand. Like that.

Isoamyl acetate, I think, which is the see-saw chemical that smells like green bananas and pears, the thing your nose grabs when it is trying to know ripeness from a distance. But that’s just me making a map a little too hard. I ask him where the boxes were stacked. There, he says, tapping the far left corner with his stick. Then he digs, small fingers at the warped luan under the counter, and pulls out a glass pony bottle with rust at the cap and a fossilized peanut stuck to the inner belly. Festas, he says with satisfaction. Sweet peanuts. The cap sticks. I coax it with heat on the copper and turn, and the room inhales.

Not one smell but a chord. Not banana, not bread, not sugar. A drift of proofing yeast that is not a scent at all but a pressure behind your eyes. A lick of hot linen from all those tea towels thrown over rising loaves. A memory of ink the way old newspapers taste on the tongue when they crinkle with your hands. The metal letter of the cash register drawer, the wool of the coat, the vinegar ghost of clean. And inside it, warm and simple as a held palm, the cliff that breaks a morning open.

Dona Lídia takes the rim of the cone and closes her eyes. She’s taller suddenly, a girl again, or maybe just as tall as the room remembers her. For a breath, time stops being a road and becomes a round charred loaf you can pick up and hold to your chest.

Later, on the curb, I label the vials: Lídia’s Cliff, Proofing Tide, Neon Skeleton, Green Banana False Dawn. A bureaucrat will rename them 14A through 14D and a tourist will pass a glowing plaque and their rental will hum and tell their nose what it ought to do. That is fine. The lattice remembers like a weak cousin. It’s not the same, but it’s something.

I go home with the day still stitched in my hair. My apartment smells like old plumbing and my neighbor’s star anise soup and the dust that sneaks in under every door. There is a box on the wardrobe that I only open when I am brave. A scarf lives inside, my mother’s. It has kept whatever the air gave it on the last day it touched her: rain, a breath of tobacco, a pharmacy’s acetone hum, the wool of the bus seat, the lavender she wore because it was cheap and ancient and good. I could trap it. I could draw out the notes one by one until loneliness was just a list.

Some maps you do not make.

I unfold the scarf and hold it over my nose until the world turns smaller and exact. I breathe. The mind is the youngest part of us. The nose is older than words. There are paths older than the city, older than the grid. We can find home with our eyes closed if we let the air lead.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
I make maps of quiet.

It started because the city would not stop talking. It spoke in bus brakes and a thousand private arias leaking from earbuds, in pigeons that beat the air like a rug and the neighbor’s radio that only played songs from a summer I never had. I tried earplugs; they dulled edges but not the restlessness. Then one evening, in a coin-operated laundromat five minutes after the last machine died, everything exhaled. The doors were glass, the lights were hum, but something softer unfurled from the corners, a hush with the weight and warmth of folded towels. I sat down on the plastic chair with a rip in it, and I don’t know how else to explain it: I could hear my own heartbeat without flinching. I took out my notebook and wrote, 9:12 p.m., Laundry King, between final spin and nightlight, a mild quiet, cotton-weight, suitable for waiting.

It went from a personal habit to a profession before I ever decided to call it one. People want quiet, like they want a decent coat or a chair that doesn’t make their back ache. They just don’t say it out loud, because it sounds abstract and slightly embarrassing, like admitting you need to borrow a steadier hand. Word travels crookedly in this city. A barista told a piano teacher who told a nurse, and now I get messages with subject lines like Seeking hush for apology, or Where do I put my sadness down? They pay me in chopped firewood, in bake-sale cash, in a jar of beach glass. Once, a man paid me in the ring from a pull-tab can that he swore brought him luck. It’s on my keychain. I have not lost a key since.

I catalog them the way birders do with swallows and finches. There’s the library-before-opening quiet, coffee-steam and paper-dust motes suspended like tiny planets, good for beginnings. The neighbor’s-porch-at-dawn quiet, with a train murmuring across the far river and the soft hysteria of a nearby squirrel, the tender kind you can set grief in without it cracking. There’s the theater-stage quiet after the last clap has settled into the seats, the curtains breathing faintly, a serious quiet, velvet deep, suitable for making a promise you mean to keep. The best ones aren’t empty. They’re textured. They carry the sense of someone having just left or about to arrive. They carry permission.

Your request becomes mine because if I do this right, I get to disappear into your need. That’s a relief some days. A woman named Mara wrote me in July. She said, I need a quiet to sit with my mother in. She didn’t say dying, but her punctuation did. She didn’t want the quiet of glass beads or hospital floors. She wanted something that would hold both their hands. I read her note in my kitchen, while my teapot began to rattle in an old-lady way. I turned the burner off, put on my boots, and went to listen to the city the way you might go to forage for mushrooms you’ve been taught to recognize: wary, reverent, hopeful.

Not every quiet is yours to take. Some are wild. The under-bridge quiet at low tide is loud with drips and gulls, but there’s a moment when the wind dies and the surface of the river flattens out like a record waiting for a needle. You can’t bring someone there; if you step too heavy, it startles and goes. There’s the stairwell-landing quiet of municipal buildings, the smell of mop water and paper towels, where sound travels in a courteous way, footstep to footstep. I thought of Mara’s mother and dismissed it. Too much bureaucracy in its bones. In the aquarium, before the school buses unload, there’s a blue-green quiet that looks like the word if you could print it on glass. Gentle, yes. But too otherworldly. We needed a quiet with wood in it.

Some searches trip a wire you didn’t set. On a Sunday morning, I stood in the back pew of a church while the choir rehearsed a hymn I didn’t know. After, they stopped. The last soprano note hung around the rafters making deals with the dust. For two breaths, there was the hush of having been sung to and the hush of waiting for your own throat to open. My mother died when I was twenty-three, in a chair by a window that had been painted shut. The silence then was a boxy thing with nails in it; it sat on my chest. I can’t map that quiet for anyone. I can only note where it refuses to go. I left the church and wrote, not here, not for us, not this time.

It occurred to me finally in the ice rink. Not during skating, not during the boy throwing his glove and the little girl laughing so hard her legs turned in on themselves. After. Lights low. The Zamboni had made its meditative procession and slid away, and there was the mirror-quiet that ice knows, a brightness folding itself inward. The benches smelled like old wood and wool hats, and somewhere a vending machine electrode clicked gently to itself. I sat with my palms open on my knees. I thought, this is a place that understands falling and getting up. This is a soft-edged earnestness. I texted Mara: Tomorrow, 4 p.m., if you can.

We sat on the top bench, two strangers coordinating our breathing like swimmers before the plunge. She told me her mother used to skate, that she had medals in a velvet-lined case once, that she kept saying the word almost lately, as if there were another line to cross, as if the next room were always a hair farther away than her foot could step. We didn’t speak for a while. The quiet settled around us and did not flinch at her tears. A man came in to fix a thermostat and nodded and left. The ice shone like a thought that had not decided to be said aloud. Mara closed her eyes, opened them, folded something small and sharp inside herself with care. When we stood, she squeezed my fingers for precisely the length of a breath.

I keep a map above my desk with strings tacked from place to place, red for the serious, blue for the gentle, green for the usefully awkward, gold thread for those that most resemble a held hand. It looks like a web or a nervous system or a plan that might work if the weather holds. Sometimes a child from the building asks me what I’m doing. Today he brought me a shoebox full of echoes he had collected from the hallway. He tipped it over and let them spill: a scoff, a sneeze, the accidental poetry of elevator ding, elevator ding. We lay on the floor and listened to the building think. He said, I like the sound it makes when you stop the faucet right before the last drop. I said, me too.

In my more grandiose hours, I convince myself I am saving something that might otherwise be lost. But quiet is not a species. It is a seam. It is where one fabric ends and another begins and, against reason, they agree to hold. We don’t earn it. We don’t deserve it. We travel to it and through it like trains moving from electric light to tunnel shadow and back again, startled each time by the sudden shift and the memory that, last time, we made it through.

When I don’t know what to do with my hands, I fold a paper crane and set it on the windowsill. The wind inhabits it and makes it nod. Across the street, a woman in a red coat picks up a child, and his laugh breaks against the glass of my apartment like a wave breaks, and then retreats. Somewhere, a laundromat goes still between cycles. Somewhere, a theater seat holds a promise in its cushion. Somewhere, an ice rink gleams in the absence of blade and applause. You could go there. Or you could just sit down exactly where you are and listen to the room finish a sentence it started long before you arrived. If you’re very quiet, you will hear the map unroll inside you. You will mark a small x and write, here, now, good for what ails you. And later, when the city starts up its perpetual aria again, you will know how to hum a second melody alongside it that no one else can hear.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
You give me a box the size of a thousand words and ask me to fill it, and suddenly I am gathering small objects from the day. Here is a shoelace that hasn’t snapped yet. Here is the way a peach yields under a thumb, the way its scent advances the room before you even lift the knife. Here is rain whispering against a screen, a private conversation between sky and mesh, while a kettle thinks out loud on the stove and changes its mind into steam.

Let there be a window, because most rooms improve with a view. Out there, a red bicycle leans against a tree with the serene confidence of things that will be needed again. The wind makes lace of the leaves. The street is named after a person who can’t remember who named it. A single sparrow rehearses a speech he will never give and still means every word.

Inside, I put a table you can write on, and a pencil that has bitten countless nerves with its sharp little teeth but never made anyone bleed. The paper is clean enough to count as hope. We will need hope, a modest amount, not the stadium-filling kind, just the kind that opens the blinds and waters the plant that pretends it doesn’t need you.

I keep a sourdough starter like a civic duty. It lives in a jar and bubbles with a quiet insistence that hunger is solvable and time is an ingredient. Yeast are patient alchemists. They don’t brag. They lift what they can. So I knead, and the dough resists, then accepts, then remembers the shape of hands. The oven door becomes a small gate of fire; I open it, and the apartment smells like the word “begin.”

At two in the morning, the radio is a boat. I turn the dial and catch glimpses of other boats, some taking on water, all lit strangely from within. Between stations is a country of static, a blizzard made of whispers. Someone in Nebraska is laughing about cows. Someone in a city I will never visit is telling a ghost they forgive them. The announcer coughs like a human, and I like him better for it.

Once, in a market, a man held up a persimmon and said, “Wait until it feels like regret.” I thought that was unhelpful and then one winter I understood. I bought three and waited until they surrendered and were better for it. We are always inventing softer answers to hard fruit. Sometimes it works.

On my desk there is a watch my grandfather repaired without tools. He was the kind of person who could hear a machine’s grievance and negotiate. He opened the back with a coin and made a small correction to the universe. When he wound it, I swear the second hand stood straighter like a soldier with a secret. It has kept time stubbornly ever since, which is either tragic or triumphant depending on your afternoon.

There is a streetlight outside that goes out and then returns as if embarrassed. I like it. It admits that shining is tiring work. Once in a while you get to breathe. Someone says it’s a sensor problem, but I prefer to think the light simply blinks at the night to prove they know each other. Names matter, even when you give them to things that don’t require them. If you name a corner “where the wind argues with itself,” you will go there when you also need to argue.

If you have ever watched ants discover a sugar crystal, you know what belief looks like. One finds it, then two, and soon there is a road made of conviction. They do not hold elections. They do not post think pieces. They simply go back and forth until the white stone is gone and the world measures out a fraction more hunger solved. I am not saying we should be more like ants. I am saying it is beautiful when a plan works.

I have kept a list of small illnesses that make me call my mother: the cough that pretends to be important, the cut that wants a story to explain it, the fear that my keys have decided to become metaphor. My mother, who believes in ginger and patience and the suspicious nature of ceilings, says, “Drink water. Lie down. If the ceiling falls, it will not be because you looked up.”

There is a neighbor whose dog wears a red bandanna and a look of someone who could talk but won’t. The neighbor waves like a flag in no weather at all. One day they left a note that said, “I like your basil.” I wrote back, “Take some,” and left scissors in a clean jar by the plant. For a week the basil looked startled and newly important.

Meteor showers get named for the constellations they seem to leak from. Perseids in August when the nights remember how to be longer, Geminids when our breath becomes a visible thought. Someone looks up and points and names a line of fire for a hero who didn’t ask to be remembered this way. Meanwhile, a hummingbird in the elm beats its heart beyond reason and finds the exact center of a flower and is not named for anything except itself.

We are good at finding patterns because the alternative is an ugly, sharp abundance of unrelated stones. I once found a receipt in a jacket I had not worn in two years and it was like a time machine no one had to patent. Bread, batteries, a magazine about houses I could never afford. I stood in the hallway and remembered a man in a blue hat who bagged my apples as if they were goldfish.

What else fits in this box? A storm that doesn’t apologize. A child who rearranges the magnets on the fridge to spell a new country. The smell of a swimming pool, brutal and clean. A cardigan that loves any shoulder it meets. The way a book sometimes closes itself when the plot becomes too much. A friend’s text at midnight that reads simply “You up” and is not a request so much as an invitation to be part of a constellation.

There are days when I believe in the mechanical kindness of the world: doors that open, buses that arrive, the exact right pocket for the coin I will need later. There are days when everything misfires, when shoelaces become snakes and coffee becomes evidence and my name feels like a shirt I should not have bought. On those days, I go to the window and try my best to learn the wind’s language. It speaks in plural and forgives slowly.

If I had more time, I would tell you about the orchard where the ladders lean like quiet prayers, about the old woman who sells buttons sorted by emotion, about the boy who learned to sew and stitched his own pockets shut to keep from losing anything ever again. I would tell you how I learned to name clouds for the things I needed: mercy-cumulus, maybe-stratus, fine-nimbus, later-cirrus.

We are almost out of room. Words cluster at the edges, shy as moths at noon. Before we dim the light, take this: a small jar of mornings you have not used. Unscrew it when the day refuses you. It will smell like cut grass and cold spoons and the minutes right before the kettle clicks. It is not a miracle. It is a practice.

And if I had one more word, only one, I would hand it to you and say: here.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The first sentence arrives like a bird landing on a wire. Light, provisional, looking around to see if the wire will hold. Sometimes I wait for it; sometimes it taps on the window and I pretend I’m not home. Today it simply perches and watches the refrigerator hum and the thin thread of dust unspool in the sun, and I think, fine, sit there, then. We can start like this, with the small electricity of noticing.

On the table there is a coffee ring drying into a map of a country I will never visit. The chipped blue bowl wears its history like a smile made of porcelain fatigue. A spoon leans against it, and the spoon is a little ship, and the ship has seen oceans of soup and storm and dishwasher clatter. Dust, here, is not neglect but a galaxy in miniature; each mote is a quiet comet burning across the only sunlight this room has. The radiator clanks in a language of a century ago, as if ghosts are asking whether we’ve kept the windows painted shut. On the wall, the calendar still says last month, a small stubbornness, the tape corner curling like a fiddlehead.

I think of the old library where the drawers of the card catalog slid with a promise, brass label frames catching the light, that particular rasp of index cards shuffling like a paper river. Your fingers learned a tender attention there: the alphabet as a topography, serifed mountains you could climb. The room had a hush you could wear like a shawl. Every book had a smell you could nearly taste, glue and dust and maybe a little sun. Now the search boxes are invisible drawers, the cards are clouds you never touch, and yet when I’m not sure what to do I still want to pull a small wooden knob and find a name waiting under my hand.

In some cities, I imagine, there’s a person who collects lost shopping lists. Finds them flattened against cart corrals, crumpled in the wrong coat pocket, damp with a parking lot’s ancient rain. Folds them along their old creases and tucks them into a tin that once held cookies. The lists tell tiny biographies: peaches, 2% milk, dog food, nails (small), cilantro if looks good, call Mom. On the back of a receipt someone wrote batteries! with three exclamation marks, as if light itself depended on remembering. Another list reads: candles (47?), cake mix, something green, something funny. A heart drawn in ballpoint wobbles next to butter. Once there’s a list that says garlic x 100 in a hand that looks entirely serious, and all day the collector thinks about vampires and feasts and someone who simply likes to be certain. On winter nights the lists get made into collages that look, from far away, like aerial maps. From up close they hum with domestic epics.

The sky this afternoon has the bruise-blue that makes everything speak more plainly. Somewhere a barometer is sulking downward. The cat—if there is a cat—becomes a little weather station, ears swiveling, tail barometrically exact. A wind picks up and the rosemary in the neighbor’s pot shakes itself awake; its smell stitches the air with something needle-clean. The man two buildings over carries a ladder from shadow to shadow like someone relocating a length of horizon. The first drop of rain is its own planet. When a storm finally comes, it shortens all distances, makes the neighborhood a quieter syllable: glass, tin, leaf, bone. Afterward, the world breathes out. The gutters clap their small applause. A worm throws itself into the light like a tight-lipped oracle.

Here’s a different kind of timekeeping: a baker tapping the underside of a loaf to hear the hollow note of readiness. In the window of a bakery I used to pass, winter would paint the glass with its mouth, and the morning line performed the liturgy of waiting. The baker wore flour like a constellation on the forearms. Not every clock ticks. Some rise and fall like bread, some measure change with the sigh of cooling crust. My father said he could tell when the stew was done by the way the steam smelled, which seems like a trick until you learn how your own house sounds when it is trying to tell you yes, now.

When I was small there was a map on the wall rug of a friend’s basement, and we knelt on the Andes and spilled juice on Australia and didn’t yet know that maps make promises they can’t keep. Subway maps are truer, in their way, than oceans: they abandon scale for clarity. They are good liars for a good purpose. I loved names more than borders, anyway: Susquehanna, Ouachita, Effingham, Larkspur. I wanted to say them until they turned sweet. Once I traced a river that didn’t exist anymore, a dashed line where water had been persuaded elsewhere, and for a long time I believed naming might be a way of bringing what’s gone a little bit closer. There are maps for grief and maps for jokes and maps where the key is just a handful of keys jangling themselves silly.

Think of a jar. Not a fancy one. The kind pasta sauce comes in, rinsed and a little ghost of tomato still in the threads of the lid. The jar is the size of a thousand words if each word were a pebble with sea-sanded edges. There is only so much you can put in. You choose: apricity, the warmth of winter sun on skin. Phosphene, the stars you make with your eyes. The word handed to you by a stranger once, lilted and wrong but generous: pronouncing basil like a title. Words are bowls, sometimes. They hold breath. They hold what we say when we have to carry it farther than our arms can manage.

On a city bus a child counts clouds loudly and miscounts and no one corrects her because miscounting clouds is a kind of innocence. A woman peels an orange into spirals as if trying to keep the rind as intact as a sentence. The scent and the sudden bright of it crosses the aisle and someone says, I haven’t had one in years, and without ceremony half the orange changes hands and the juice is on their fingers and they are strangers in the way that people can be precisely themselves and near. The driver arrives at a pothole he has known since autumn and rolls the bus over it like a drumbeat, a reminder. He says at stops, Take care now, because it is a small, servicable blessing, because the route is a kind of lullaby he can sing even when nothing else in the city holds still.

I keep thinking how the mind is a refrigerator door cluttered with magnets that do not entirely agree with one another: a postcard with a lighthouse and a recipe card in a dead aunt’s slanted hand and a school portrait in which a child looks like she has been interrupted just before she becomes herself. Lists lose their tacks and drift under the stove. The storm cleans the street and leaves us a little brighter, a little brusquer. The drawer in the library sticks unless you lift it first, like a secret. We are, mostly, a chorus of small reliabilities, and when one of them works—a light clicks on, a bus stops, a loaf sings hollow—we are surprised each time by the kindness in it.

If you need a task, here is one. Put a jar near the sink or the door. At the end of each day, write down one brief, bright thing and drop it in: the way today’s wind found the chime, the letter you finally mailed, the absence of the thing you feared all morning. At the end of a season, pour them out and read them back. You will have made a map not of where you went, exactly, but of what you noticed. That might be enough. That might be the bird returning to the wire, looking at you as if to say, this is the place, this is the line, this is where we land.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
I begin with a blank window and the hum of a refrigerator, a small ship’s engine keeping time in the harbor of a quiet afternoon. The cursor blinks like a lighthouse, insistent and tender, issuing permission more than orders, as if to say, write, I’m here, keep going with you. Outside, someone drags a trash bin that rattles like marbles, and a dog, indignant at daylight’s audacity, declares jurisdiction over a square of sidewalk today. I think about the tiny economies of mornings, the exchanges of glances, steam, spoons, passwords, coins, apologies, and how they add up to imperceptible civilization. There’s a plant on the sill, stubborn and forgiving, which I have neglected and praised in alternating seasons, and it survives both kinds of attention. Memory arrives like laundry, warm, folded, carrying the scent of a place I loved before I knew to name it, doorframe marked with pencil lines. I was small, and the world was impossibly vertical; adults were mountains whose pockets contained maps, crumbs, and mysterious keys that jingled with private constellations. Now I am taller than some trees I once believed were ancient, though the soil reminds me, patient and gritty, that counting rings requires cutting. I prefer observation to surgery, so I watch shadows articulate the hours across the table, gentle hands turning pages only light can read aloud today. Coffee cools into second opinion, less dramatic than steam, more honest than hype, advising patience, patience, which is difficult medicine to swallow without sweetness today.

The room keeps time with radiators and neighborhood sirens, a percussion section arranged by engineers and misfortune, scored for distance, urgency, and someone else’s routine. There is always a list near my hand, items that masquerade as obligations but are really invitations, small doors opening on a corridor of generosity. I consider walking, because walking is my favorite punctuation, the comma that keeps sentences breathable, the semicolon that suggests we are not finished, gathering relevance. On the corner I will meet the bakery’s air, warm with sugar and ambition, and I will envy croissants for excelling at layered vulnerability daily. If the sky is undecided, clouds bartering across blue, I will accept their negotiation, because weather is democracy conducted between oceans and patience of land. A bus sighs at the stop, ancient creature pretending to be practical, ribbed with advertisements promising flawless skin, instant recipes, and retirement as a hammock. Everyone boards with ceremonies learned from repetition, the graceful ballet of passes and bags, the quiet apology of knees, the mutual recognition of shared destinationlessness. I have carried sadness like a suitcase I refused to unpack, thinking permanence safer than discovery, until a friend pointed the handle’s grooves in palm. We laughed then, not because it was funny, but because laughter is a bridge you can throw across silence when you are tired of wading. I walked home lighter, even though nothing had changed except the willingness to place the suitcase down, which is itself a small revolution for today.

Writing feels like that sometimes, a setting down of weight you mistook for architecture, discovering the room was portable and you were carrying its ceiling. There is embarrassment in relief, as though admitting burden exposes naivety, yet muscles learn grace by releasing, the way doors learn hospitality by opening daily. A neighbor waves from across the alley, holding a paint roller like a torch, redecorating courage in room the color of unassuming fruit and optimism. We are all repainting something, if only the edges of our attention, making room for new light to land without apologizing for the dust today. I think about the word forgive, which is sneaky, because it sounds like giving first, a preemptive gift offered to future so it can breathe. Maybe that is why stories comfort me, because they forgive in advance, allowing characters to err extravagantly while trusting a path will appear through thicket. When I cannot write, I clean a drawer, astonished by paperclips reproducing in the dark, rubber bands dreaming of weather reports, expired coupons practicing humility. I find a photo of people I loved into distance, whose faces keep laughing without me, proof that joy does not require witness to proceed. This is comforting and cruel, a weather pattern with sun and hail, reminding me to hold gratitude with two hands and let it drip anyway. I still believe in attention as a form of repair, not fixing by force, but noticing until the unnoticed reveals a hinge, then opening gently.

The world is not waiting for me, but it keeps including me, the way a choir includes breath between notes, audible if you listen kindly. Sometimes inclusion is a bench without plaque, made available by absence, the previous sitter having risen and left warmth behind like a signature that fades. I like benches, their unpretending democracy, the way they ask nothing except occupancy, and even then, they forgive you for changing your mind mid-sit often. A train passes somewhere I cannot see, its sentence punctuated by crossings, machinery insisting onward, onward, which is sometimes wisdom and habit dressed as destiny. I remember a teacher who said curiosity is disciplined wonder, and I have tried to practice that, trimming questions without pruning the roots of astonishment. It helps when I fail to be spectacular, because ordinary projects invite companionship, and grandeur tends to hire seclusion as a consultant with business cards. So I make soup, call my parents, fold towels like field notes, and feel knowledge accumulate in the pantry where spices alphabetize themselves slowly overnight. At night, the ceiling reveals its cartography, invisible by day, and I recognize shapes that migrate, because plaster has dreams it pursues when lamps surrender. Tomorrow will bring emails shaped like pebbles, skipping toward me across a pond of expectations, each ripple an appointment, each splash a request wearing shoes. But for now, I listen to the refrigerator’s faithful ocean, the lighthouse of the cursor, and the laughter still happening without me, blessing everything anyway.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
There’s a small key in the bowl by the door that I don’t recognize, with a nick on its shoulder and a smile of brass. It sits there the way forgotten objects do, pretending to be patient, not making demands, but growing heavier in the imagination. I turn it in my palm and try doors in my head. It doesn’t fit the mailbox. It doesn’t fit the attic. It might fit a drawer with letters I never mailed, a locker from a summer job, a music box that gave up its song and kept its silence. I’d like to believe it opens something lost at just the right size, like a moment.

Outside, the neighbor is hosing down his stoop as though the dust is a thing with history that must be persuaded to leave. He wears headphones and mouths a chorus I can’t hear. Every few seconds, he glances at the sky, nervous in a way that would be endearing if I didn’t recognize it. The clouds have that torn-paper look you get before a change. A gull slides through the alley of rooftops, dramatically unconcerned with our terrestrial arrangements. Watching it, I think of travel in the way people think of travel: the looking more than the going.

On my kitchen counter there is a jar of honey that refuses to crystallize, a small miracle of stubborn liquidity. Bees gather their gold from thousands of tiny mouths and the result is a sweetness that outlasts the flowers that inspired it. I could say this is how memory works—scavenged, concentrated, resistant to decay—but memory clumps and clouds; it invents its own winter. This morning I stirred coffee until the spoon tapped the cup like a conductor calling the strings to readiness. The coffee tasted like a story I’ve heard before and am happy to hear again.

I remember the first time I noticed that doors breathe. Pushed open, they sigh. Pulled shut, they make a soft apology. In a school hallway, a teacher once said, “Write the thing you can’t stop thinking about,” which, at the time, was the way my mother folded towels, corners meeting like conspirators. Today it is the key, the honey, the gull, the fear that the finest details of a life go unremarked until they are only dust, until there is no one left to notice that the dust had a history and a texture and a name.

When I was a child, I tried to keep a list of everything. I had a notebook with blue whales on the cover, and in it I wrote: things I saw from the car window, things my father said at red lights, the color of rain when it landed on a baseball diamond, words that made my mouth feel foreign. The list went wrong quickly. The everything of everything is a tide without a calendar. I learned to be satisfied with driftwood: a phrase here, a scent there, the startling, incomprehensible fact that a grasshopper is mostly muscle.

A friend calls to tell me about a minor disaster that rearranged their kitchen and their marriage. Stove, spatulas, vows: all evidence that heat wants company. We talk about grout and grief, about whether grout can be a metaphor for grief, about how sometimes the smallest line of molding keeps a room from falling apart. I say a platitude and we both make a joke of it. I am not sure if friendship is a series of well-aimed jokes at the edge of loss or if it is simply showing up with a dustpan when a glass shatters. Either way, there is sweeping.

There’s a plant on the sill that was supposed to be easy. “You can’t kill this one,” the nursery woman said, and then gave me the look people give a person they suspect might find a way. The plant drooped, then revived, then drooped again, a soft green cardiogram of our shared uncertainty. I rotate the pot the way nurses rotate patients, to avoid bedsores of light. Every time I consider giving up, the plant produces a new leaf that looks like a tiny flag for a country I don’t recognize. I salute. We carry on.

At the bus stop down the block there is a small sticker that says: You are exactly where you are. This would be more comforting if the bus schedule weren’t so honest. People bring their mornings with them: a thermos, a quiet hungover dignity, a damp collar. There is a man who feeds the pigeons whole bread rolls, which is an act of either cruelty or faith depending on your position in the story. The pigeons handle it like professionals. If the city had a soundtrack, it would be a thousand small chitters and the unsubtle drum of delivery trucks.

Sometimes I think the reason we invented windows is to practice wanting without moving. A window lets the eyes roam while the body obeys its chair. It’s a training ground for desire that is also a school for contentment. This is how we become the sort of people who can love something and not destroy it, or can love something and still let it change. I think about this when I watch the neighbor, whose hose has become a snake skimming a river. He laughs at something only he hears. The gulls make their old, rude jokes.

I would like to tell you that I have discovered some binding principle, some reliable geometry that makes it possible to step from this thought to the next without losing my footing. But the day insists on its own argument. A receipt falls from a book. My phone buzzes. There is a hairline crack in a plate that I put my thumb against and feel, in that tiny ridge, the whole tectonic history of clay. The key is still warm from my hand, though it sits, innocent, dumb. Warmth is a way the body leaves an answer where no one asked a question.

When I can’t sleep, I count things I am certain about. The cat is asleep, a loaf with ears. Somewhere in town, a bakery is deciding the morning’s croissants. The moon has a crater named for a person who believed mathematics was a story about longing. In a drawer, there is a postcard with a picture of a lighthouse, which belongs to all of us who have ever needed to be told where the rocks are. Certainty does not multiply easily, but it is not extinct. It glows like a dial on a bedside clock.

Maybe the key opens a lock that isn’t a lock yet. Maybe it belongs to a door I will only build if I believe in it. I think about carrying the key with me, making it a talisman, asking it to be a witness. I think about placing it on a park bench, starting a story in someone else’s hands. I think about taking it to the hardware store and asking the quiet, competent person to make a copy, so that whatever it unlocks, I can be sure not to lose. Instead I put it back in the bowl.

There is bread. There is honey. There is a gull worrying the horizon. There is the neighbor, done with the hose, standing with his hands on his hips as if to say: we did what we could for now. There is a plant flagging in a corner and a bus that will arrive later than even its own shy estimation. There is a plate with a crack and a clock with a glow. There is a window that does not require an answer, only a gaze. There is, as always, a beginning wrapped snug in the middle of an ordinary end.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
I find the house with weather inside after the funeral, when the casseroles have cooled to a skin and the people who knew what to say are a little hoarse and beginning to watch the clock. I park outside the old bungalow with its porch that sags in the middle, take the key from my pocket like a charm, and step into an entryway that smells unreasonably, impossibly, of summer rain.

It’s sunny outside. On the blacktop the heat rises in a visible quiver, and yet here, in the dim hallway where our school photos still go crooked no matter how straight you hang them, the air carries petrichor and the green-hum of a storm that has not yet arrived. The hair on my arms lifts. I set the keys on the little table with the basket for mail and the spare flashlight and the jar of marbles my mother said she kept to remember how the light could be held and scattered at the same time.

She was not famous for it, though she once predicted a blizzard by the taste of a hospital elevator and the accountant at church swore she could find mushrooms by listening for a hush in the ditch. She collected weather in the same way she did buttons, stamps, the gummed corners of envelopes: without making a fuss, with the slow gravity of a person who thinks in decades, not days.

“Come over when you’re ready,” my aunt had said on the phone. “There’s more than you’ll expect.” As if I could expect any of this. As if grief didn’t make everything ache like weather in the bones.

The kitchen is August as remembered by tomatoes. A ceiling fan slow-rotates a dull coin of light. Heat gathers benignly in the corners, holding the things it warmed—formica, the drawer of tea towels, the dented tin where she kept recipes written in a handwriting that got tired so gracefully you had to admire it. I open the pantry, and the small glass world breathes.

Shelves floor to ceiling: jars and bottles, vials the size of medicine and the size of thunderheads. Each neatly labeled in her blocky print on masking tape. The closest ones say, January Fog, 2003, and Sleet Off the Lake, Night Shift. Farther in: Campfire Smoke Caught in Her Hair; Afternoon Wind Between First and Second Kiss; The Frozen Morning We Drove to Get You; A Summer With No Rain, Saved Piece By Piece.

I pick up a jelly jar with a screw-top lid. A bubble of something quivers inside it—a luminous, pale density, as if a ghost had been rubbed thin and placed under glass. The tape reads: First Day of School, 1997. When I open it, the room shifts. The air is fidgety with pencil shavings and breath through wool. My tongue remembers the taste of dry crackers eaten too early, the way a backpack strap chews the shoulder.
