When I was hired—I would say more accurately that the shop found me—I asked the previous keeper who decided which days were worth keeping. All of them, she said. Even the ones you swear you don’t remember. Especially those.

The day I never inventory waits in the back, in a cigar box that still smells of cedar and damp matches. I think of it sometimes while dust rises from my sleeves like moths. It is the day the phone rang at the wrong hour and the voice said my father’s name in a way that made it a place I couldn’t return to. I worked through the hours like someone counting coins that belonged to someone else. I wrote a list of practical things and then lost the list. The fish tank bubbled without asking. The mail came with a catalog for boots I did not need. I peeled an orange and the oil stung skin I did not know could sting. I did not go home; I did not not go home. The day hung on a peg at the back of me and I ducked through doorways not to brush it.

Years pass like the dust that settles and unsettles in the slant of light. I have watched people take their days in the crook of an elbow or press them to the forehead like a fever cloth. I have slid the latch on the door when the warm dark arrives. One Tuesday I give in and take the cigar box down. My hands behave like mine and also like hands I have borrowed from someone braver. The day inside has creased itself to fit. It lifts up easy as a clean sheet. It smells like oranges, yes, but also like the drawer where my father kept folded maps, the ones with pencil stars in all the places he wanted to stand and breathe.

I think the day will hurt in a straight line, but it opens the way an old road opens, with more curves than you remember and fewer potholes, as if the last years have been doing their quiet sewing. There is even laughter, rude and badly timed, at something the nurse says and I say back. There is the pattern on the curtain, a blue that is more sky than hospital. There is the fish tank bubble, which I have upgraded in memory to a brook. There is the call that goes through everyone you love like a spoon striking crystal, and the way the sound keeps ringing after you put the glass down.

I do not spend the day. I lean it against my ribcage the way a person leans a broom before stepping through a door. I let it ring and ring. Then I slide it home, to a place I have mislabeled, and I write a new tag: Forgiven.

On Tuesdays I pass my hand above the shelves and feel the small weather rise to meet me. I check the latches. I rotate the days that bloom in darkness. I label one more box with a blue pencil that has lasted longer than pencils should. You would think it would dull to nothing. It has learned to be lengthened by use. So, I think, have I.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
You have given me a thousand words the way someone hands a pocketful of seeds and says: see what grows. I could line them up like corn in a neat field, straight rows of thens and therefores, but the day has a slant to it and the wind pulls at sleeves, and I keep thinking about the small fly tapping at the window. Light pulls it forward and glass says no, the ancient loop of desire and boundary. The tap is tiny but insistent, softened by the hum of the refrigerator and the slow tick of a clock I swear speeds up around people I love and slows down in waiting rooms. I am supposed to write, but the fly is writing too, in its own Morse, and I do not want to interrupt its sentence.

I remember a bus that announced its stops in a voice that sounded soft with sleep. The driver had a feather clipped near the mirror, a bright bit of blue that wobbled on potholes and rain-slicked curves. A woman across from me slept with her mouth open and two fingers still pinched on the strap of a paper bag that read Thank you in cheerful red loops, and I wanted to tuck a napkin under her chin. That was years ago. On that same day I kept a cup of chamomile warm in both hands and called my grandmother, who told me a joke twice in a row and laughed both times as if it were new. Memory is a kind seamstress but she is not precise; she will take in and let out the fabric of a day, pin a different button where one was missing, and send you out looking presentable.

I wonder why I’ve been thinking of maintenance a lot—how hero stories get the statues, but the quiet, daily acts keep the whole world from unfastening. Someone gets up to salt the steps before dawn. Someone wipes the public sink with a paper towel. Someone pulls out a splinter and then returns to the onion, weeping in a way that can be blamed entirely on dinner. There is a kind of love that is a steady hand on a latch and a list taped to the fridge that says: call the dentist, water the fern, apologize. The fern, trim as a banker, goes about its green arithmetic while you fumble through voicemails and check boxes and the day gives you a gold star for still being here.

Today I watched a video of starlings turning the sky into velvet. They call it a murmuration. A storm of birds and yet no crashes, no squabbles parked in midair; they belong to that strange intelligence of crowds where nobody is in charge and yet everything coheres. We like heroes, but the world is full of competent middles: slime molds finding the shortest path to food in arrays scientists make to look like subway maps, ants dragging leaves in bright triangles that could be bunting for a parade. Somewhere a programmer writes a patch note that prevents a tiny bug from eating all the memory, and no one claps, but the city keeps running and someone’s coffee order goes through and the elevator stops on the right floor.

One night not long ago I walked past a laundromat glowing like an aquarium. Inside, people floated in the blue, parted by rows of round doors, socks and shirts tumbling like thoughts in a warm brain. A child pressed both hands to the glass and watched a red fitted sheet make a planet with two pillowcase moons. Across the street, someone in an upstairs window practiced trumpet, a sound that began fat and ended shy, and then tried again. On the corner a cat held court on a stack of paper towels in a bodega, eyelids at half-mast, a degree of royalty that would be arrogance in any species less perfect. The register printer chirped like a small bird. The man behind the counter moved with a dancer’s economy. I bought a nectarine, which was both too hard and somehow just right, and bit into the future and its mess of juice.

Some days I crave instructions. How to oil the hinge of the hour so it doesn’t squeal as it swings. How to keep basil alive after August. How to excuse myself from spirals and choose a straighter thought. Breathe, people say, and yes, but also: count the cars between two green lights and forgive each of them. Decide that the dust is evidence of time well wasted. Put a spoon in the freezer in case of morning regrets. Store passwords like treasures. Write your name inside your coat as if the world were still small enough that a stranger would bring it back to you. Learn which maps take you into experience and which keep you safely above it. It turns out neither safety nor experience is the only gospel; sometimes it’s enough to notice you are in your body and that your body, improbable as dawn after a terrible night, is still negotiating with gravity on your behalf.

I am haunted by the thrift store shelf of orphaned mugs. Each with a slogan no longer relevant to a life, a chipped lip, a handle designed for someone else’s hand. But what a soft power they hold: an ordinary cylinder that conducts heat and story. My favorite mug has a hairline crack that’s been stable for six winters. We have an agreement and a middle distance affection. I do not pour boiling water directly on it. It does not break under the shock of being suddenly needed. If I’m honest I have avoided friendships that felt like sleek, new travel mugs; I like the ones where you can see the repair, the line of gold someone used to put a life back together when the pieces didn’t want to understand each other anymore.

Here is something that comforts me: so much of our bestness happens in the middle of forgetting how to perform. You laugh with your eyes closed at a dumb pun. You sing wrong lyrics with confidence and become, briefly, a new lyric entirely. You hand a stranger a dropped glove and say nothing as if the whole act were of no significance, even though in the cold it is the difference between numb and bearable. You misread a sign—No exit as Now exist—and then for a whole block the world glows as if it were subtle and kind on purpose.

There are larger concerns, obviously. I read the news; some days it feels like trying to hold a burning pinecone. But even then there are people in scrubs holding hands for a few minutes past their shift, someone at a radio station queuing a song that will lift the corner of someone else’s sorrow. A neighbor who waits for you so the elevator door doesn’t close. The thrill of a new word learned at 3 p.m.: crepuscular, say, the way the day puts on its dusk fur. I try to make a habit of saving a seat for my future self on the other side of worry. I try to write her notes in margins: feel free to be wrong; you can edit later; leave the porch light on.

If you asked what I was trying to do with your thousand words, I would say: set out bowls of water along the paths where animals run at night. Small offerings, in case thirst finds them. In case it finds you. Not grand sentences, but a few useful ones you can tip into your pocket and forget until they warm to your thigh and reveal themselves in some fluorescent aisle, a new kindness suddenly possible. Meanwhile the fly has stopped tapping; perhaps it found a seam in the screen, or perhaps it is tired, or perhaps it has learned to be content with the idea of sky.

I finish this with a chipped mug beside me and a slice of nectarine that refuses to stop being summer. I think of you somewhere, maybe on a bus, maybe in a kitchen lit like a promise. Between things, which is where most living happens. May your hinges be quiet. May your plants forgive you. May the trumpet upstairs keep practicing. May someone save you a seat and, when you arrive late and out of breath, may you find your name still there, inked in an ordinary hand.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
At this hour, the street is a long exhale. The city hasn’t decided what face to put on yet, and for a moment it forgets to perform. A delivery truck sighs, a bicycle murmurs past on soft tires, and the sky gathers pale intentions in the east. The air smells like metal and damp bark. Somewhere a baker opens a door and looses warm sugar into the cool, blue dim. A cat considers me from the curb with the suspicion owed to those who walk without purpose. I tell myself I’m going to the corner for bread but really I’m going nowhere, letting the morning fit itself around me like an old coat.

“Whatever comes to you,” you said, and so I let the words arrive as they wish, like gulls that think every palm is a shoreline. A memory knocks: my grandfather’s radio on the workbench, its back open like a book, the tubes glowing with their glassy, insect-heart light. He breathed in the sharp rosary of flux and solder, nodding as if listening to an invisible choir. When he turned the tuning dial, the world unspooled: stations sliding like fish under a boat. A voice from Ohio, a trumpet from Havana, static like rain crossing a wheat field. He taught me to find stations by feel, to hear the difference between noise and the thin, stubborn thread of a human. “If you’re patient enough,” he said, “there’s always a song.”

Patience is a kind of love. Bread requires it, and seeds. My grandmother pressed tomato seeds into soil with her thumb, like blessings. She talked to the plants as if they were shy guests who might need another invitation. In August, we would move through vines sticky with their peppery breath, palms mottled with green. We salted slices over the sink and ate them standing, caught in that brief window where summer tastes like its own handwriting.

The corner shop has a bell that sounds like an apology. The baker is already flour to the elbows, his apron declaring what he will not admit about the morning’s labors. I buy a loaf that feels like a small, sleeping animal in my hands. On the way back I pass chalk drawings on the pavement: hopscotch squares annotated with impossible numbers, a sun with fifteen unsteady rays, hearts with names. The rain will smudge them later, but for now the chalk glows like a promise made and believed.

When I was young, I found a brass key in the back of the junk drawer. It was too ornate for any lock in the house, a tiny theater of curls and dents. I wore it on a string until it turned my skin green. I kept it through three moves, certain it belonged to a door I had not yet seen. Later, I learned that some keys are made not to open but to remind you that closing isn’t the only action a hand can make. Even now it lives in a bowl with screws and pennies and a die that only ever rolls fours. Sometimes, without thinking, I take the key out and turn it in my palm, measuring how much of my believing is habit and how much is hope.

The sea is nowhere near this street, but it keeps visiting my mind like an uncle with stories. I have a photograph of a lighthouse lens in a museum: a monstrous glass chrysanthemum, all concentric circles that bend light and insist on its usefulness. Imagine being that clear, I think, designed to take whatever brightness the day gives and throw it farther than it thought it could go. In fog, the horn keeps time for those who cannot see. I used to be afraid of fog—the way it hides even gentle things—but now I think of it as a slow clock, a way to move by listening.

Time misbehaves anyway. On my dresser is a watch with a cracked face that stopped at 3:12, a time that feels like a shrug. When I’m late I tell myself I live by that watch. It’s a small rebellion, to appoint one’s own hour. A friend of mine sets all her clocks seven minutes fast, neatly outsourcing her better self. We do what we must to meet ourselves in doorways, breathless and half laughing, pretending it was all part of the plan.

A man once dragged a piano down our street with a rope and two friends and a sense of occasion. He stopped at the corner and played Rachmaninoff as taxis parted around him like bright fish. Children gathered, their scooters abandoned in a clatter, and a woman with a bag of oranges closed her eyes. People in high-visibility vests clapped at the end as if they had been at Carnegie all this time. Afterwards he sat on the curb and peeled an orange, the segments cupped in his dark, elegant hands, as if to thank the morning for being there to catch the notes when they fell.

It matters, I think, to make a little theater for the day, any day. A note taped to the fridge: Drink water. A tulip in a jar beside the sink, an extravagance that costs less than coffee and refuses to apologize for existing. A stone you turn over each time you leave or return, a small vow. It matters to say hello to the cat and pretend you mean it. It matters to be foolish about small, durable things.

We speak a lot of beginnings as if they were doors and not thresholds. In winter, my friend dyes yarn in a pot the size of a wishing well. She lowers skeins into indigo, waits, lifts, waits again as the blue oxidizes in air and deepens—ultramarine, the word itself like a ship. “Color is time you can see,” she tells me, and I nod as if I knew it before she spoke. The yarn dries in strings like rain. Later, someone will wear that time, knot it into their own day.

What is there to say about endings except that they happen whether we rehearse them or not. A radio turns to static, a plant goes yellow overnight, a key’s teeth smooth away with the romance of use. I am trying to be less dramatic about it, to let things end without narrating their nobility. The other morning I watched a flock of small birds move as if they were one thought, dark punctuation in the sky. They didn’t know who was leading. They didn’t take attendance. They turned because the turn was in them.

There is a quiet argument on the page between words and the white that holds them. The space is not absence, it’s part of the sentence—the breath you take so the next thing can be said. I want to leave a little space here too, to honor what does not arrive, what waits its turn. If you tilt your head you can hear the next thing clearing its throat in another room.

On my way back, the cat has chosen a patch of sun as if it invented the idea. The loaf bumps against my hip. The city begins to remember itself: a bus yawns awake, a window goes up, somebody’s phone vibrates against a tabletop and is answered with a tired hello. I think of my grandfather’s dials, the pursuing hum, my grandmother’s thumb pressed into earth. I think of the piano’s stubborn, splendid insistence that a corner can be a stage. I think of you, requesting whatever comes, and of the thin thread we could call a song that is maybe just attention with its hair down.

I cut the bread when I get home, and the crust sings the way crust does, surprised to be traversed. Steam lifts like a weather update. I eat a slice thick and plain, because some mornings that is the only honest thing to do. The day can adorn itself later. For now, there is this: a key with nowhere urgent to open, a watch forgiven for being wrong, a bell that apologizes and means it, and the patient, absurd, necessary act of believing there is always, if you’re willing to listen for it, a station worth tuning to.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
You hand me a small plate: 1000 words. Enough for a modest meal, not a feast. So I will set out what can be cooked quickly and well, with a little salt and a little flame, and invite you to eat with your hands.

First I want to say that the world seems better from the middle of the night, when the refrigerator hum becomes a soloist and the street is a glassy river. At two in the morning, a maintenance crew on the bridge wears orange vests like small suns, their cones forming a shy geometry. Work lights turn steel into bone. One of them, the one with the clipboard, whistles an old song and thinks of nothing he would trade for this hour. If you’ve ever driven past and felt a sudden tenderness for strangers bending to their tasks, you have already understood more than news can tell you.

In the kitchen sits a mug that has been repaired twice. The crack is a lightning fork, thin as regret. It never breaks in the same place. It has learned the routes of failure and avoids them now. The handle impresses its curve into the palm, and there is always the little trance of steam between your face and whatever the day requires.

There’s a jar by the door that catches all the foreign coins and old keys. If you shake it, the past rings. Not like a bell, not so definite. More like wind chimes made of decisions. A key without a matching lock is an artifact of trust that has gone elsewhere. Sometimes, on a hard afternoon, it helps to plunge a hand into that cold metal lake and hold something whose use you’ve forgotten. It gives you permission, briefly, to also not know your own purpose with any sharpness.

Weather radar taught us to read emotion in color. A smear of green is manageable, a yellow band asks for patience, red is trouble, purple is theater. We refreshed the page so often during certain summers that refresh was the act of faith: that new information might change the storm already on the way. There are other radars we keep refreshing. Text chains. Bank balances. The system status page with its neat line of green checkmarks like sprouts that could fail at any time.

Once I put a grain of sand beneath a cheap microscope and found it was a translucent shard, a mountain flake, a cathedral window’s lost eyelash. The beach is full of such small cathedrals making a noise like applause. This is what makes me mistake the finish line for the starting gun, again and again: everything is more intricate the closer I look.

At dusk a flock of starlings gathers over the grocery store and does the old magic we never get used to. How do they do that? we say, as if wonder required method. They turn as if inside a single thought. Sometimes I think that thought is hunger, and sometimes I think it is not hunger but the memory of it, a choreography they rehearse so they won’t forget what saved them.

Many drafts are bravest in the folder where no one will see them. The unsent note that says I’m sorry in three different keys. The memo with the sentence that is too direct to survive the meeting. If we ever opened all our drafts at once the room would bloom with versions of ourselves that nearly were. There is mercy in what we never send, and mercy also in what, mercifully, slips through anyway.

There’s a baker in the dark whose day begins when ours still has its shoes off. His hands lift dough that breathes like a small animal. Flour drifts onto his forearms in constellations, makes him look like the night sky rolled up his sleeves. He folds and turns, folds and turns, an action older than explanations. Later you will break a roll and the steam will ghost your fingers and you will not think of him, which is part of the bargain everybody makes with everybody else.

A blackout quiets the refrigerator. The house holds its breath with you. Through the window the apartment across the way is suddenly a theater of silhouettes and murmurs. The night regains its star-speckle and peels at the edges. You light a candle and become briefly medieval, an acolyte in socks. You find the flashlight, it works. You place it face-up on the table so the ceiling becomes a pond of light with one bright fish. These are the evenings when the stairs meet their neighbors by flashlight, and names bloom like night-blooming jasmine.

Seed libraries are the softest revolution we have. A drawer of envelopes hand-labeled with words that read like spells: Blue Lake, Mortgage Lifter, Moon and Stars. Inside, they rattle like small arguments for the future. If hope is a verb then this is one of its ordinary conjugations: to save, to share, to plant, to wait, to taste.

There’s a plow that moves through the city before dawn and pushes winter back into piles at the curb. The driver has a thermos that tastes faintly of last spring’s coffee and last fall’s paint thinner. She is listening to a station that only plays songs from the decade when she learned to parallel park. The blade throws sparks against a manhole rim, a tiny meteor shower. The whole machinery of municipal care happens where we might have dreamt a flood.

Tidepools make promises and keep them twice a day. Bend to look and you are someone else for a minute, a visitor to a glass city. There are animals that have not bothered with bones. There are worlds that run on fable rules and still manage to pay the bills of hunger and threat and dawn. Your reflection is there too, rubbery, wobbled by a snail’s decision to move.

People on trains learn apology in many languages of body and throat. I’m sorry is a shift of weight, a half-smile, a hand lifted and then put away. The announcement says there will be a slight delay and the whole car adjusts its internal math. Watch the shoulders resign, the fingers text, the novels re-open. Watch the person who lets someone else sit because that is the kind of math they do.

If you are grieving, I will not offer metaphors that tell you your loss is a season. Seasons repeat. Your loss is a shape in the air where a voice belonged. Some days it is a door so heavy it breaks your arms to move it, and other days it is a thimble you carry in your pocket and touch to remind yourself that the world permitted such a craft to exist. This is not advice. This is just me setting a chair near the one you are sitting in, and staying.

In the drawer of misfit electronics there is always a cable that belongs to something with a vanished mouth. We keep it, as if the device might come home, panting, eyes bright, ready to be plugged back into our care. On days when the news is a mountain, I untangle cords until they lie without knots. It is a job that obeys. It suggests other knots might listen.

A lighthouse that no ship consults anymore still works without complaint. Its beam continues its long slow swipe like a custodian’s broom. The ocean doesn’t care and cares entirely. There are structures that wake whether we need them or not; some of them are in us. I am thinking of the way your lungs check the air while your brain is busy writing lists.

The best bell on a bicycle is the one that sounds like a laugh. It is not an order. It is a asking and a thank-you in one. You ring it as if to say: let’s avoid being a small accident together. Most days this is what civilization amounts to.

There is a town where a library keeps a map with a torn corner. Every child who borrows it learns that the missing piece is a lake that still exists. They fill it in from memory now, a smudge of blue, sometimes too round, sometimes a sideways heart. That tear teaches them detours before they can spell the word. They grow up with a suspicion that the best way rarely travels straight.

On winter mornings breath writes on the glass and the sentence dissolves before you can read it. This is the kind of message I trust. It appears because you exist, it vanishes because time exists, it leaves a space you can fill with whatever courage you can manage before the kettle whistles.

I have reached the end of the small plate. If I could, I would tuck in a sprig of parsley, as restaurants do when they want to say finished without saying done. Instead I will say this: the museum opens whenever you notice. Admission is whatever you have in your pockets. The exhibits are not rare. They are right here, within hand’s reach, murmuring their patient names.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
On the third floor of the municipal museum, where the plaster flakes like dandruff and the fig tree in the courtyard has long ago learned to thread its roots through broken tiles, there is a room with a single north window and a hum of old refrigerators. In that room, I catalog small winds.

I do not mean the kind that ruffle dresses along promenades or trouble the skirts of thunderstorms. Those belong to everyone and no one. I mean pinches and pinwheels, drafts caught at doorjambs, the sigh that slips between two lovers at the end of a long argument, the last breath of a candle. I mean winds too shy to raise dust.

They arrive in envelopes torn from water bills, in baby food jars with labels half-peeled, in antique vials someone rescued from a chemistry lab two demolitions ago. There’s always a note, hurried or ceremonious. I pin them in the ledger. I dab a bit of sealing wax to keep them from slipping loose.

Mrs. Karcz, retired lighthouse keeper, sent me eight breathfuls from the cottage where her mother used to churn, saying the kitchen still turned windy when she sat down to mend. A schoolboy taped a grocery bag around the exhaust fan of a ferry and posted me the bustle that collected. A beekeeper brought a swarm’s breath, all velvet and fret.

You laugh, perhaps, at the romance of it. You wonder what one can do with a drawer of trifling currents. I am not immune to ridicule. When the city cut funding, I was moved between departments like a painting that no longer suited any wall. But the winds waited. They do not spoil if you are kind.

Some days the room smells like damp pockets and others like peppermint lozenges sucked dry. After the storm three summers back, someone left a crate of gutters full of trapped spirals the color of zinc. I wore gloves, because storm winds hold onto splinters.

I didn’t start as archivist. I was inventory in the archaeology wing, counting clay feet and volcanic glass. Even then, I leaned toward the light. Artifacts are heavy; they resist you. Winds, though—the lightest thing a person can be faithful to. I began to help in the room, then stay late, then sleep there like a cat in a warm box.

If you were to visit, I would show you the cases. Over here, the hospital wing: toddler fevers blown into paper cups, the whistle off a pressure cuff, the ripple that runs before a curtain. Over there, the kitchen precinct: oven sighs, huffs from kettles right at the point of singing, the brief weather made by opening a fridge door.

I keep the saddest winds in a shoebox of unpaired gloves. I keep the angriest in a red gas can with the cap nailed shut. A laugh is quick to bottle—a hand over a mouth. A sob is like trying to catch minnows with your palms. Most grief escapes and that is properly so.

This would have been enough, I think, to spend a life sorting whispers, making labels, writing down who sent what and when. For excitement, there were always moths. You learn to accept a quiet horizon. But last winter the north window changed everything. It stuck.

It had swelled, the wood bedraggled from a week of rain. No matter how I wedged my shoulder and cursed, it held fast. Outside, the courtyard fig scraped, exhaling itself in little torn ribbons. Inside, the hum of refrigerators turned impatient, like a throat cleared and cleared again.

The first bottle slipped right off the shelf and shattered. You will say that the glue failed, that the shelf was warped. You will be right. Still, the wind that came out was a long, cold seam, and it stitched itself under the door and went looking for stairs. Another bottle fell—a spring classroom draft, chalk-dusted and sly. It sniffed the light bulbs, interested.

I could have called security. I could have swept glass, taped, apologized. Instead I took a key and unlocked the red gas can. The angry winds boiled up, bright and blood-hot, and they met the cold seam and combed themselves into a useful ribbon. Together they lifted the stick of the sash. The window wheezed open.

We should pause here for the rules, such as they are. Winds have temperaments but no morals. They love to be assigned. They hate to be ignored. Praise makes them fizz; contempt turns them to knives. If you tell them what they are, they will demonstrate you right. Try to make them do housework and they sulk under furniture.

With the window open, the room drew breath like a swimmer breaking surface. I laughed without bottling it. Outside the courtyard darkened to blue ink and the fig’s leaves tilted their coins. All along the shelves, the little winds took notice. Some tested their corks. A few rattled like mice in seed bins.

In the days after, people sent fewer. I wondered if the world had gone still. I bicycled to the river and found sailors beating the water with their caps, begging for breeze. On the hill, turbines turned like tired ballerinas. The flag at city hall drooped its chin on its chest. Something had paused the larger engines.

In my room I opened drawers and boxes and cans. I coaxed from them what they were willing to give, and I carried them in cupped hands to the window. One by one they went out: a book-page flutter, a stairwell hush, the bracing breath before a speech. I do not know if they matter in the scale of fronts and isobars. I only know the fig began to clap.

Later a girl knocked and asked if the museum had air. I said yes, a little. She wanted some for a kite that had forgotten its letters. I poured her a teacup of hallway drift. The kite went up like a blue word remembered.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
I am thinking about the click a kettle makes just before it boils, that small premonition of steam, and how some days feel like that moment forever, a hush before something ordinary that still feels like wonder. The window is open, and the light is the color of a peeled apple. Somewhere a truck backs up and issues three patient beeps, like a bird practicing caution. I suppose I should write about something important, but importance seems like a hat you put on when the mirror is watching. What comes to me instead are small, precise scenes: a coin lodged in sidewalk tar, a grandmother opening a tin of buttons, a tidepool that smells like iron and sky.

Once, on a bus that smelled faintly of oranges and raincoats, I watched a girl read a paperback with a broken spine. She licked her finger to turn each page, and the motion was the same as blessing bread. Across the aisle, a man clutched a box of pastries as if it contained a fragile animal. I remember thinking that the city was a machine for producing private rituals, each of us a little priest of our own obscure faith. The bus lurched and sang on its shocks. Someone rang the bell one stop too early and did not get off, and it felt like a sentence that almost ended and then changed its mind.

In the house where I grew up, the attic had a window like a secret coin stuck in the roof. In summer, hornets floated there like discarded commas. We stored keepsakes we could not quite keep: school projects made from sugar cubes, dresses outgrown and waiting for imaginary sisters, a computer that hummed even when it was off, because memory, I learned, can be noisy. My mother kept recipes in a wooden box with cards that smelled like cinnamon and ballpoint ink. I loved the cards that began with the names of other people, as if their hands were folded into the batter. Alma’s cake. Bernice’s cookies. The past made edible by allegiance.

I have a friend who believes that every photograph is a negotiation with loss. He says the camera is not a thief, it is a notary public; it does not steal your soul, it witnesses the shape it had that day. He once took a picture of his father’s hands and later could not remember the sound of his father’s laugh, but those hands remained, a pair of parentheses around silence. We looked at the print together, held by the same edge, careful not to leave fingerprints. The skin was mapped with pale rivers. A gold ring shone like a vowel. How little we ask to remember. How easily memory turns water into stone.

Meanwhile, outside, the garden has become a democracy of unruly citizens. Tomatoes crowd the trellis like gossiping uncles. Basil goes to seed, astonished by its own ambition. A snail draws cursive across the flagstones, patient as a chronicler. When I water, the soil drinks loudly, a grateful animal. Bees lurch among the lavender, drunk and devout. There is a spider whose web is anchored to the mailbox, a choice both practical and poetic: all news must pass through hunger. I consider relocating the web for kindness, then do not. Kindness, I remember, is sometimes letting an architecture stand.

I do not know what to do about the news. It arrives like weather, but it is weather that has learned to speak, and it never says, go outside, the air is full of mercy. I want to know the names of rivers more than the names of scandals. I want to ask the cashier how her mother is doing and not feel like kindness is a fragile thing we smuggle across borders. Some days I carry a reusable bag with nothing in it, as if preparedness could be empty and still noble. Some days I am nothing but a teapot, a promise of steam.

At night I sometimes dream very small dreams. I dream that I am tying my shoes and they keep becoming different shoes, as if the day cannot decide what kind of walking it will require. I dream that a house sits at the edge of the ocean and the ocean is trying to teach it a song. I dream that a long table is set under a tree, plates waiting, and all the chairs are empty but warm.

Sometimes I compose letters I will never send. Dear person whose laugh knits the air back together, I witnessed you today in line at the post office, and the way you thanked the clerk made the room a kind of chapel. Dear person who picked up the worm from the hot sidewalk and moved it to shade, I hope someone relocates you gently when the sun is too much. Dear person I was ten years ago, keep that blue shirt; it will matter later, not for how it looks but for how you will forget yourself in it at a party and talk to a stranger about clouds until the music changes.

People say time is a river, but I think it is a pantry. Things go in, plainly labeled; things come out altered, without warning. A can of peaches becomes a memory of a porch in July. A bag of flour turns into a winter afternoon with friends and a table dusted white like first snow. Sometimes you reach for salt and touch instead the jar where you keep the dead moth your child insisted on saving, its wings perfect as glass. The shelf is always a little messy. You promise to organize it on Sunday. You promise to forgive yourself Monday.

If I have a thesis, it hides in the lint trap of the day: gather what is soft, remove what would burn. There is no grand ending, only the kettle’s small announcement, and the way we pour, and share, and call that warmth enough for now.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
By the time an envelope reaches our table, it has become louder than paper ought to be. The trays arrive each morning, a shingled sea of corners and stamps, and we tip them into neat storms: things without return addresses, things with rain-bled ink, things addressed to “Granddad, by the sea.” Our blades whisper, our gloves matte the oil of fingerprints into neutrality. We are the last chance, the slowest current. We slit along the edges so carefully you might think we were surgeons—or thieves—except it is tenderness we are after. We are looking for the missing parts. We are always looking for what might let something arrive.

The day the suitcase showed up, Mara set it on the sorting table and blew dust off the buckles like she was waking a spell. The thing had been left in an old postmaster’s attic, she said, mislaid among heating ducts and a stray badminton shuttlecock. When we cracked it open there was the briefest hush, as though the room made space. Inside, bundles tied with twine. Postmarks from years when stamps still felt like small promises, their serrations delicate as eyelashes. Airmail blue, onion-skin thin. The room smelled like paper and attic sunlight.

I work the L—M tray, but the letter was on top, out of order, addressed in a hand I recognized before my mind agreed. A thin envelope, light enough to float if a window opened. My name written the way it was when the city still had clanging streetcars and I thought I would never leave Apartment 5B on Linden. The stamp was domestic, the ink a brown that wanted to be black. The return address was my old street. The sender’s name was my mother’s. She had been gone five years. The date in the corner was smudged, but the month was the one with thawing roofs and sidewalks gritted with salt.

Policy says we can open only to find a better address. Policy didn’t foresee the feeling of your own name stepping toward you from behind time. I slid the blade along the envelope’s lip and felt the resistance break. A pressed leaf slid out first, its veins as neat as handwriting. The paper crackled, tired but unbroken. It began, my dear—she had never written that to me, only said it when laughing, brushing hair from my face with a wet hand.

“If this finds you,” she wrote, “it means I was brave enough to mail it. The power’s out. I can hear the neighbors speaking softly as if they’re in the same dark room. I am making bread by hand because the mixer is sulking, and I wanted to tell you what I know. When you press your thumb into the dough and it sighs back at you, that is the time to stop. Not sooner, not later. Bread is a letter the heat reads. The oven is a kind of post office. What arrives depends on what you entrust.”

There was more: small news from a life I had already outlived. The way the late-day sun fell in stripes across our linoleum. The gossip of the woman with the red bicycle who had been brave enough to shave her head. A storm memory: the night I was little and a tree fell, and the house hummed with battery lamps and we counted between lightning and thunder until the windows decided they were drums. Tucked into a corner of the page with a paper clip was a key wrapped in tissue. One of those small brass ovals you keep in a dish by the door. On the tissue, in pencil, she had drawn a tiny flower and the word toolbox.

When my shift ended, the city had gone gray with the kind of rain that doesn’t mean anything yet. I took the letter and the key home like you carry a sleeping bird. The blue steel toolbox had followed me through three apartments without consent, a thing you can’t throw away because its weight is full of former selves. It had been my father’s, or the idea of him, we never said. I put the key to the lock with a small ceremony I had not planned, and it turned with surprising courtesy. The lid opened. Inside were a jar and a folded sheet of lined paper and the faint smell of old metal and lemon oil.

The jar held beige flakes, curled and light, tied into muslin with red thread. The paper had recipes in my mother’s hand: measurements given like advice—flour by handful and by weather, water by what the dough asks for. Under that, a paragraph separate from the kitchen: “If this finds you,” again, “then I have been braver than I was. When you were small I told you he went away because going was easier for a child to hold than vanishing. He didn’t go. He didn’t know how to swim well enough. He was gentle, and the water did not care. I am sorry for letting you listen for a whistle that wasn’t coming. There are some truths I kept until I learned how to say them without drowning. If you want to forgive me, feed this. Call it what you like. If you can’t, feed it anyway. It will forgive you back.”

I had forgotten that naming is a way to keep things. Bread starter can live a hundred years if kept warm and fed: an inheritance made of hunger and time. I read the instructions twice, heat blooming up my throat. Warm water, small spoon of honey if you’re feeling sweet about it. Flour, a handful. Stir until it runs slow like a ribbon. Leave it somewhere it can overhear the house. In the morning it should be breathing. If it isn’t, talk to it, try again. Patience is a heat all its own.

The starter woke like a polite guest, expanding in a bowl that had once held cherries. It smelled faintly like apples, like a porch after rain. I fed it the way I wish I’d fed letters I never sent. I was awkward with the kneading at first, elbows asking permission of the air; then the rhythm found me. The counter freckled with flour, my hands branded with tacky crescents. Thumb presses, palm folds, the slab of dough returning my question with a patient push. Naming it after the sea felt both foolish and correct, so I whispered a syllable only I would remember.

When the loaf went in, I stood with my ear to the oven door, listening to the transformation begin. The crust blistered and then blistered more, scoring opened like hidden laughter. I tapped its side when it came out and the sound was what my mother promised, a hollow knocking, as if there were someone inside asking to be let out, as if the bread were trying to recall the word door. Steam braided into the kitchen light. I didn’t realize I was crying until salt touched my lip and made the crust sweeter. I have never believed that timing is mercy, but here, with a loaf talking to cool air and a letter settling into itself on the table, I felt something loosen: the thread that had kept certain rooms closed.

Back at the depot, the next day’s envelopes were no less lost. Someone wrote to “The person with the green coat I saw at the station,” and I slid it aside with care, as if it could feel being set down gently. What we send and what arrives are rarely the same. That should depress me; it doesn’t. The gap is a kind of meadow where strange things root: a key waiting in a box, a truth that needed years to learn its vowels, a recipe written in a light that no longer exists but still illuminates.

I have started writing letters I cannot mail. To the woman on the red bicycle. To the man in the next building who practices scales when the air is blue. To the past versions of myself who made choices without recipes. I fold them, I put them in an old shoebox, I feed the starter, I tend what insists on living. In the evenings, the bread whispers its small percussion and I listen, practicing patience like a language. There is a sentence I keep saying to the empty rooms and hearing back, not as an echo but as advice: every delay is also a form of timing. I believe it because I am holding proof in my hands that the long way around can still be a way home.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
The cobbler’s shop smelled like soap and pine and the old denominational scent of leather, warm and grainy as a fresh loaf. Nails the size of punctuation marks lined the benchtop in a neat grammar: commas for the corners, dashes for the heel plates, a handful of exclamation points when a sole came in peeled back and shouting. The bell over the door had long stopped ringing—its spring lost, its tongue tired—but customers still announced themselves with the clatter of need, with an embarrassed softness, as if to admit that a thing you trusted had failed you required a lowered voice.

Mara sat at the bench with an awl in her palm like a pencil. Her tools had family names to her: the edge-trimmer was Luc, the burnisher was Tamsin, the rasp was the cousin who always disagreed but got the job done. She did not call customers by their first names unless invited; they rarely asked for hers. Mostly they said Miss, or Ma’am, or that little incline of the head reserved for people who know the underside of our days. I have a job interview, they’d say. There’s a wedding. My father’s funeral is Tuesday. I walked this pair hard during a year I don’t want to talk about. She’d nod and say, We’ll make them steady, and she would, because steadiness is a thing you can stitch if you take your time.

When she warmed the glue until it went from pearl to honey and brushed it along the roughened leather, the shine sometimes captured a flicker—like a cloud in a puddle. She told no one about this. When she eased out an insole, the powdered grit beneath clung to the shape of a life, the way old paper keeps a pressed herb, a season of green made flat and remembered. Pressure patterns spoke like tide lines. Left feet kept secrets differently than right. Rubber recited less than leather, but still, there was a murmur.

She learned early to look and not look. A man’s boot offered up the abrupt glare of bicycle spokes in summer sun, a hotel hallway’s carpet moons, a sea of hospital tiles. A woman’s sandal held a smear of pollen, the composition of an upscale grocery store’s fluorescent light, a dog’s backward glance at a crosswalk as if checking that love was keeping pace. A child’s sneaker chirped and clicked in laughter until her throat hurt. Embedded in a brogue’s welt was a single pebble from a mountain trail, and the relief when it was finally removed felt like a thought released.

One afternoon in late March, a boy brought in a pair of white sneakers still arrogant with their newness. The soles had rashy scrapes, the uppers a nick or two. They were too quiet in her hands. Not empty—emptiness has a cold whistle—but quiet like a room that has been washed and is waiting for a bed to be moved in, for the first imprint of a head on a pillow. They smelled not of street or sweat but of rain that could not make up its mind.

“They’re for my sister,” the boy said, one hand still hooked in his backpack strap as if he’d run here. “She won’t come in.”

Mara stepped to the doorway and saw her across the street, standing on the curb in bare feet. She had her arms folded, not in defiance but the way you wrap yourself when certain breezes are too much. The pavement pressed its cold into her carefully. The boy—maybe twelve—watched Mara watching, like this was a test he didn’t know how to pass. “She hasn’t worn shoes since,” he said, and stopped. Since was a cliff-edge word. The shop accepted cliff-edge words the way it accepted worn treads: you set them on the counter, you let them be there, you get to work.

“What needs doing?” Mara asked, back at the bench, weighing one sneaker, then the other. The boy’s shoulders dropped, grateful for the ordinary. “They hurt her,” he said. “They make her feel like she’s being kept.”

Mara turned one shoe inside out as far as its design allowed, inspecting the stitching that ran like the borders of a country. “We can soften them,” she said. “We can teach them gentleness.” It was not something she had said before, and yet there it was, true in her mouth.

She thinned the counters where they bit at the heel bone. She cut a footbed out of cork and leather—cork that held warmth like bread and memory like bark. She stitched with tea-dyed thread, a small softness the color of afternoon. On the underside of the lining, where no one’s eyes would go but where a sole would always press, she cross-stitched tiny constellations—five, seven, nine stars—arranged in shapes that had names in some other sky. The act felt like tucking a note into a lunchbox, like pulling wool through time.

While she worked, she kept her own old red flats under the bench so tight to the wood they couldn’t sigh. Those shoes had no laces but many knots. They had carried her over a lake on a ferry made of afternoon. They had stood on ceramic tile while a kettle stuttered, and the kettle had not finished what it began. They had gone with her once to a place where geese stitched Vs into the wind. She avoided slipping them on, avoided the hunger of them. Sometimes denying is a craft.

By the time dusk fitted itself into the windowpane, she had rebuilt the white sneakers to be less insistent. She wrapped them in brown paper and tied them with waxed string, which always made her feel like she was trusting the package not to tell anyone what it was until it arrived.

The boy returned alone the next morning, bouncing on his heels. “She won’t take them,” he said in a rush that made the bell-less doorway seem, for a second, to ring. “School starts Monday. They said she has to have shoes. I told them but they said.”

“Wait here,” Mara said, and walked into the street with the parcel, her apron still on, her awl still in her pocket like a talisman that could persuade the world to be softer at the edges.

The girl stood where she had been, as if the curb were a dock. “No,” she said when Mara held the parcel out. There was no temper in it. It was a fact like temperature.

“I won’t put them on you,” Mara said. “We can try something else.”

She set the package on the curb and eased herself down beside it, knees making the conversational noises of age. “Stand on my feet,” she said, pointing to her socks. “Just for a breath.”

The girl looked at her brother, at the traffic light muttering to itself, at Mara’s face as if trying to locate a hook that would snag her. Finding none, she put one foot, then the other, on top of Mara’s. Her skin was cool and trembling. Mara felt, through the cotton, the delicate machinery of arch and bone, the untranslatable poem of a foot that wants to be trusted by ground.

They stood like that. A bus sighed past. A pigeon performed an argument with a crust and won. The girl breathed. She cried in the way one might lean forward into a downpour and realize it is warm. “They won’t keep you,” Mara said softly. “They’ll remember with you. You can teach them.”

The girl looked down at the parcel. She slid it toward herself with a toe and held it as if holding a small animal that would accept her if she did not ask anything of it too quickly. “Okay,” she said, which is the bravest word when it is whispered.

After they left, Mara untied her apron and, alone at the bench, put on her red flats. She braced and let the angle of the old instep return to the angle of her body. Memory rose not as a flood but as weather—the long, grey, honest rain of a season that was called by its true name. A ferry deck, an arm waving that she had sworn not to forget and had forgotten, the quiet of shelves where two cups sat always side by side just in case, the way winter mornings hurt and healed at the same time. She took her awl and stitched a single thread through the strap—no repair necessary, just a visible line, her own secret constellation.

Months turned in their simple way. Summer walked up and set down its bags. One afternoon in July, the girl came in, no bell to ring, a drift of air and sunlight ahead of her. The white sneakers were white no longer; they had met grass stains, puddles, the indecency of parking lot oil. They were alive. “We crossed the bridge,” she said by way of greeting. “My brother showed me the place where fish pretend to be shadows. I can stand there now.” She placed on the counter a small square of paper with a pressed clover inside. “For your shoes,” she said, and grinned, and was gone.

People kept bringing their pairs, pairs meant to accompany them into rooms small and large, pairs for late shifts and first dates, pairs that would walk them out of houses they’d thought would never empty. Mara put on her glasses and listened with her hands. Sometimes she left stitches in the shape of asterisms only she named. Sometimes the thread held a color like tea, like dusk, like riverbeds. She kept, in a jar by the register, a teaspoon of grit from every sole she had cleaned—less superstition than geology, a study of how the city wears us and we wear it back.

She knew, because her work made it obvious, that we do not go places so much as we meet them. Surfaces are headstrong but forgiving. Leather keeps our confidences, and rubber, despite its reputation, is tender in the right light. She was no priest and no witch and no saint of second chances. She mended. She returned. She pressed, with care, the bottoms of people’s days up against the rest of their lives so nothing gapped and no stone could slide in where it didn’t belong.

On slow afternoons, she would take off her red flats and set them on the bench as if admitting them as customers. She’d put a finger on the worn toe cap and feel the engine of time, precise and unhurried, cinching and releasing as it pleased. Then she would lean back, close her eyes, breathe pine and glue, and in that breath map a small exhale, a geography no atlas wants but everyone needs: the road between here and the next step, the country of standing up again, the little republic of soles that know your name.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
At the end of the line, the trains stopped three summers ago. The benches still remember the weight of knees jiggling at departure boards, but the glassed-in booth collects only heat and dust now. When the transit authority sent an email asking for volunteers to maintain the old station, I typed yes before the cursor could blink. The keys arrived in a padded envelope with a stale peppermint rattling at the bottom. No instructions. Just a suggestion to clear out the lost-and-found.

The cupboard smells like wool and rainy days. Inside, the objects don’t huddle so much as lean, the way strangers lean without meaning to when a train car sways. I carry them to the ticket counter and begin a catalogue I’ll never send to anyone. Not an inventory to prove what exists, but a way to say: you were here. You, or your things that learned your touch.

A cardigan the color of seashell insides, buttons smooth from years of decisions. The cuffs are stiff with salt as if its wearer once leaned against a ferry rail and let the spray christen them. In the left pocket, a ticket stub to a museum exhibit about whales. In the right, a river-worn pebble, thumb-shaped. I hold it and think of a person trying to make time skip, flicking a stone across a current, counting the skips and not the distance.

A black umbrella that opens into constellations. Someone thought to replace the usual nylon monotony with a night sky; Orion blooms over your head when it rains. The ribs of the umbrella creak like knees. I stand beneath it in the empty station, watching raindrops drum stars and slide away. It feels like rehearsing for a meteor shower, or like standing inside an answer to a question you forgot you asked.

A thermos, dented, with the stubborn smell of bergamot. Its lid screws with a soft plastic squeak, the kind that fades when a thing is loved. I picture a hand twisting it on a platform in winter, breath white and edges sharpening, the world briefly held at a sip’s distance. Someone refilled this, and refilled it again, thinking they had time to refill it tomorrow too. I pour the last invisible tea down the drain and feel like I’m tipping something sacred.

A lime-green dinosaur, no bigger than a matchbox, its paint rubbed off along the back ridge where a thumb always traveled. It is a tired stegosaurus. I line it up on the counter’s edge and it leans as if bracing for impact, which is exactly what children do before joy. Somewhere, a kid cried on a platform, inconsolable, while adults negotiated with phrases like you’ll get another and it’s only a toy. I hope the kid is older now and treasures other things with the same ferocity.

A brass key fob heavy enough to make your pocket hang lower on one side. It’s stamped with the name of a hotel that’s been renovated into digital check-ins and plant walls, the past replaced with a pleasant and trackless present. The key’s teeth are uneven, like a skyline. I like keys that insist on being carried, on making you notice you are opening something. This one sighs when I drop it on the counter, a small bell without sound.

A wallet slick as a flat stone, empty of cash, fat with membership cards and apologies. Inside is a torn square of paper, a stanza handwritten then struck through and started again. The words keep returning to the verb keep. Keep going. Keep watch. Keep nothing. There is a water ring where a coffee mug once was. I don’t know if the poem was ever finished, or if the person decided the act of erasing was the poem. I tuck the paper back, resisting the temptation to finish it for them.

A scarf the color of new leaves, threaded with the smell of rosemary and smoke. It’s generous enough to be a shawl if you forgive it. I drape it over my shoulders and my reflection in the dusty window looks like a traveler arriving after the storm. Some objects insist on being worn to be understood, and then they ask you to remember where your neck ends and the world begins.

An old camera with no memory card. When I lift it to my eye, the viewfinder cuts a rectangle out of the landscape. The platform becomes framed and almost manageable. There is satisfaction in the click even though it records nothing. I think of all the shots I have not taken, and how sometimes the act of looking is more faithful than capturing. If you look at something long enough, it begins to look back, and you have to decide if you’re ready to be seen.

A paperback that smells like sunlight and damp cardboard. The spine is broken precisely in the middle, as if someone made it to page 153 and then let the story hold their place until the next life. Pressed between pages is a sprig of lavender, flattened and flaking, a dried gasp. I read the sentence it bookmarks, and it is not remarkable except that out of context it seems like instruction: Go home by another way. I return the lavender, feeling complicit.

A left glove, knit, navy, with a loose thread like a comet tail. The left hand is the one we forget to offer. The glove has a stain that could be ink or berries. I slide my left hand into it and it fits almost but not quite, which is as close as a lost thing can come. Somewhere, its partner has forgiven it, or hasn’t noticed yet.

At the back of the cupboard: a folded train schedule printed on heavy paper for a date that never arrived. Delays in tidy columns, times marching forward toward a morning permanently postponed. There’s an authority in timetables, their disciplined lines promising that if you align yourself, the world will meet you where you agreed. When the trains stopped, no one announced that the promise had dissolved; it just thinned into normal.

On my lunch break, I sit on the bench that still holds the imprint of impatience. Through the grimy glass the tracks gleam like cooled scars. A moth wobbles against the window and twitches in a light only it can see properly. The station clicks—metal cooling, wood settling—like a house remembering how to be empty.

People say you can tell a lot about a person by what they leave behind. I am not so sure. Some things get left because a hand is full or a thought is heavier than a handle. Some are flung away like seeds, hoping to root elsewhere. Some are offerings without an altar. The cupboard feels like a chapel of wrong turnings and gentle failures, and I find myself treating each object like a confessional—listening to what it knew when it was held.

The last item, not in the cupboard but in my coat pocket, is a name tag with my name written in marker that has given up at the edges, the letters feathered and soft. It’s from a wake where people who loved the same person briefly loved each other because of it. I wore it so that strangers could say my name in a voice that rose like a question and fell like an answer. I slid it into my pocket when I left because I couldn’t bring myself to leave any part of that room behind.

I add the name tag to the counter, to the constellation of what once was nearest to someone’s skin. The adhesive has collected lint; it doesn’t stick anymore. I write the date on the back anyway, as if that pins it better than glue. Then I start a new page in my notebook and give it an index number it doesn’t need.

What is the point of this catalogue? To prove I came? To delay the ache of gone? To make an arrow pointing to a door that no one will open? Maybe it is only this: to sit in a place designed for thresholds and practice staying. To open umbrellas indoors and call down stars. To wear other people’s warmth and drink the ghost of their tea. To look and look until the empty platform looks back and says, gently: there you are.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
In the city where the river moved like a long thought, there was a shop with no sign, only a window and a door and, behind both, the patient thrum of ticks. The locals called it the hour-mender’s, as if the hours were trousers that needed hemming, as if time were a sleeve you could darn. People tried to explain the place to visitors and couldn’t. They wound up pointing and saying, Listen. When you got close, the sound changed from ticking to breathing, and you felt, slightly, that you were the one being wound.

Inside, the shelves held clocks that would have made any museum jealous—bronze carriage clocks, enamel dials, a wooden cuckoo with a beak smoothed from affectionate thumbs. Mixed among them were stranger things: a jar of winter silence, marked with a label in a looping hand; a shoebox of spare seconds that had fallen, apparently, through suit jacket cuffs and train station floors and settled here, like coins in a wishing fountain. The hour-mender had tweezers for minutes, a brush for dust finer than dust, and a magnifier he wore as if it were a monocle and a thought.

People brought him their broken time. Some of it had faces and hands; some of it was invisible and a little shy. A fisherman came in with a pocket of knotted afternoons, complaining that the light on the water wouldn’t let him be alone. The hour-mender sat with him, palm to palm, unpicked the knots like burrs from a dog’s coat. A schoolteacher handed over a bell jar in which a Monday had been thrashing for years. He lifted the lid; it went quiet. “No charge,” he said to her, and took only a story in return.

That was a rule of his. Money made time meaner. He would take it—rent had its clock—but he preferred stories, and buttons, and small birds that fit in the left coat pocket of the day. Another rule: he could not restore what had already been spent. He could not return you to a hallway in a hospital, or a sidewalk under rain, or a kitchen where a kettle had just begun to let out its steam. He could ease the hinge. He could stop a room from stealing from you so fast. He could lend. And there was a third rule, which he didn’t write down because it wasn’t his to keep: borrowed time carried the lender’s rhythm. You felt the other person’s breath on your sentence, tasted their tea in your mouth. It made the gift honest and slightly strange.

One autumn, a woman came. She was the kind of tired that lives behind the eyes and holds a cup with both hands so it won’t leak out. Her fingers smelled faintly of mint. “I need an hour,” she said, as if you could order one the way you order thread or a prayer. “Between three and four, Thursday. I lost it.”

He thought of his rules. He thought of the wall clock above her head, which had a way of putting its seconds on their tiptoes. “I can’t take you back,” he said gently. “There is no back. There is only, sometimes, a place beside. I can lend. But it will be mine.”

“What’s your hour like?” she asked, and he found himself grateful to her for thinking to ask. Most people wanted time like bread.

He took down a jar not much bigger than his fist. This one had a ribbon. He hesitated, then he took up a pen and crossed out the word sunrise. “It tastes of pear and cheap coffee,” he said. “It has the sound of the first bus. If you hold it wrong it will become a bird and leave.”

She looked as if a bird would be fine. “My mother,” she said, and paused, and he knew the rest. He had been given that sentence many times, from many mouths. He unknotted the ribbon. The room seemed to step aside. He watched her slip into the hour with the grace of someone who has been practicing all her life for a single conversation. The clocks listened and kept time with the bed in a white room across the city, the lift of a chest and the fall. Everything was ordinary. That was the miracle.

When she came back she had lost something around the eyes and gained it in the jaw. She handed him a ceramic sparrow whose tail had been mended with gold. “She made this when her hands were steadier. I’ve been saving it for something fair.” He set the bird on the shelf by the jars of silence. “That hour was dear,” he said, because he believed in saying true things out loud. “And worth every second,” she answered, because stories sometimes rhyme.

The next mornings were thinner. He poured tea and forgot to stop. He reached for a shoelace of time and found it coiled somewhere else. The city’s sirens seemed a notch too loud, as if they were trying to pull him forward by his lapels. He did not regret the hour, but he felt it absent the way the body knows when a tooth has gone and the tongue cannot let it be. He caught himself replacing the missing with jokes, and then apologizing to the kettle when it boiled into lonely.

That winter the river went glassy. People brought him frostbitten minutes, blue at the edges, and he held them in his palms until they pinked and wiggled. A boy came with a diver’s watch. “It was my dad’s,” he said, as if the verb could warm itself enough to be the present. There was sand beneath the crystal, maybe, and a hair. The boy wanted the seconds to go faster. He wanted to turn eighteen by Friday and drive away from his mother’s crying and the sudden way dishes learned how to be loud.

The hour-mender told him the truth. “I can file the burrs so they don’t catch on your clothes,” he said. “But I won’t make quick out of you. Fast makes thin.” He gave the boy a pendulum anyway, one that slowed first snowfalls and the head on a sleeping dog. “Use it on good things. The rest, run through. Close your eyes, sprint, open them again. That is a skill.”

He supposed he had always been giving small things like that. He liked the way people left with their pockets heavier and their shoulders lighter. He liked when a minute forgiven made a person kinder to themselves. He liked the quiet that broke sometimes into laughter. He liked that time, when mended, ticked more like a heart.

In spring, the river got ambitious. It climbed its banks like a child up a chair leg and peered into town to see what else might be made of moving. You could feel it from anywhere you stood: a low grammar under the day. He put the bottom-shelf clocks up high and put the jars in the window so the light could look at them. When the water came, it entered careful as a cat and then forgot itself and insisted. A dozen seconds bobbed like corks. The cuckoo said, unhelpfully, that it was three.

He was ankle-deep when she returned, the woman with the mint-hands and the golden-tailed bird. She had a child now, asleep against her rib like a song you don’t know you’re humming. “I brought you something,” she said, and held out a small tin whose lid was dented in a friendly way. He opened it and tasted lullaby. It was one of those hours that aren’t on any clock: nothing special and inarguably necessary. He could have taken it. He could have topped himself up and made his mornings fat again. He shook his head. “Keep it,” he said. “Spend it. Break it. Stain it. That is what hours are for.”

They stood and watched the river inspect his tools. On the shelf, the sparrow held the room in place. He thought of rules, and of where rules failed, and of what you owed the hours that had held you without complaint. He took a piece of cardboard and wrote, Back in five minutes, and laughed at himself for being old-fashioned about it.

In the street, the city shrugged and glittered. People hurried with blankets and buckets and the kinds of jokes you tell when the world insists on being wet. The hour-mender sat on the step and let his feet be cold. He closed his eyes and spent, lavishly, a minute on nothing but listening. The river and the sirens and the pigeon-shoulders and a far-off train. The clocks behind him kept his place. When the minute was gone, he could tell. He could also tell it had stitched itself, neatly, to the hem of the day. He liked the way the seam showed if you knew where to look. He went inside and got back to work.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
Imagine a room that doesn’t exist until you enter it. The door is ordinary, painted the color of dry bone, and the knob is warm as if someone recently left. When you push inside, the air pauses. Dust and late afternoon light trade favors across the floorboards, and the corners hum like old radios. On a table sits a glass of water that reflects nothing but a blue that belongs to oceans you have not seen. The chair knows your name. The ceiling clears its throat. Someone, possibly you, has already written a note and folded it into a bird.

You sit without thinking, which is to say you surrender a decision to gravity. The wood creaks in a language you do not know but secretly understand. The note bird watches with a paper eye, then unlearns itself and becomes a sentence again. It says, Start where the light is honest. You laugh, or maybe the window laughs and borrows your mouth to do it. Outside, a tree rehearses concern. A siren skims the city and forgets to return. On the water glass, two fingerprints face each other like continents remembering to drift. You wait, and waiting begins to explain.

Listen, the ceiling says, we have borrowed you for an hour. We would like to show you the machine that stamps possible futures until they curl from heat. It is quiet work. We oil the cogs with music, and when songs fail, with tears. We take returns without proof of purchase. We are not saints, only patient. The machine gulps at nothing and gives us a little something back, the way a shoreline accepts a thrown stone and returns a rounder stone later. You ask what happens to mistakes. The corners hum again. Everything, they answer, eventually becomes texture. Here.

You tell them about the week your shoelaces kept untying themselves. You worried the world had grown tired of knots and decided to reconsider the idea of togetherness. You tell them how a stranger on a bus handed you a peach and said, For later, in case later needs sweetness. You saved it until it bruised. You ate it anyway, and it tasted like doors of summer. The ceiling nods, the chair nods, even the dust nods, though no one expects dust to agree. The machine clucks, motherly. You realize there is more kindness in rooms than streets. Most days.

The door behind you never closed, but somehow it also locked. You do not mind. The room proposes a small exchange: your caution for its memory. It promises not to gossip. You accept, because there is an invisible itch under your ribs where questions hatch. The room remembers a farmer who carried lightning in a jar and fed it to his fence each spring. It remembers a book that dreamed its readers into better versions of themselves. It remembers a violin with a crack shaped like a map. It remembers the taste of pencils. It blushes. So do you. Now.

Outside the siren returns, panting. It leaves a radius of quiet like a peeled fruit. Somewhere, someone signs a form that promises an outcome impossible to deliver. Somewhere else, two people build a bridge between their knees on a couch and cross it very carefully. You think of the ocean that taught your lungs restraint, of the mountain that once tattooed your feet with blisters shaped like stars. You think of your mother, who kept recipes she never cooked because paper is a form of hope. The corners brighten, then dim, a heartbeat you almost trust to borrow. For now.

There is a drawer you have not opened. It waits with the patience of glaciers. When you finally tug, it sighs out a cold laugh and offers what it has. Inside are buttons without coats, keys without locks, maps without legends, and names without owners. There is a photograph of a lake that never happened. A small note says, Return what was never yours, especially the heaviness. You pull heaviness out by the handful. It turns to moths and haunts a lamp you cannot see. The drawer thanks you for traveling light. The chair claps once, very softly. In approval.

You ask the room what it wants from you now. The room admits it is bad at requesting, excellent at receiving. It would like a story you have not told anyone, not even yourself, which is difficult, as you are particularly good at disguises. You decide to try. You tell the room about the time you pretended not to love a person because loving seemed expensive, and afterward you spent years paying anyway, coin by coin, with no ledger to explain it. The ceiling nods through each confession. When you finish, the door unlatches. No one says congratulations. Good work.

You stand, and the chair lets go, a damp leaf releasing rain. The water in the glass has learned your reflection and wants to keep it. You drink anyway, so your face becomes a weather that passes over your tongue. The room looks smaller suddenly, or maybe your certainty has taken its coat and left. Outside, evening braids itself to the city. The street remembers how to be river. A stranger’s laugh travels like a kite with no string. You fold the note bird again, set it on the table, and it flutters slightly, as if practicing courage. For you.

Before you leave, the corners ask for a promise. Carry us, they murmur, when you forget the compass and remember only the storm. You agree, because agreeing is a way of planting seeds in the pockets of your future coat. The door is easy now. The knob is cool. The hallway outside smells like pencils bitten in school and rain rehearsing on windows. You do not look back, not because you are brave, but because you want the room to breathe without your constant witnessing. On the street, a bus arrives. You board. The peach pit in your pocket wakes.

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
On the fourth floor of the old municipal building, past the cracked mural where a river used to be painted blue, is the Registry for Misplaced Intangibles. No one meant to put it there. It accreted the way lichen does, over decades of people standing in echoing hallways with their hands empty and their mouths full of words like almost and nearly and I can’t quite remember. Someone made a sign, eventually. Someone else brought a bell for the desk. The bell sticks at odd hours and rings itself when the heaters kick on.

We keep our shelves low and our windows high. Light comes in the color of paper. There are no locks, but there are rules in a coil-bound notebook with a frayed elastic that no one can ever find when they need it. Rule 3: You may not claim what belonged to someone you never were. Rule 7: Once returned, a thing will fit differently than you remember. Rule 1 is in pencil and always smudges. It says, simply, Be kind.

People bring in envelopes, boxes, sometimes only a story. We label their losses with string tags and a number, and we put them where the air feels right. Misplaced flavors are in drawers with sachets of coffee grounds to keep them from wandering. Lost plots slink under the radiator and lick their paws. We’ve had to put the unasked questions in call bells to keep them from dissolving into the floorboards. They ping when no one is looking.

On Mondays the line is long with weekend misplacements. An elderly man comes up in a coat that has remembered his shape. He takes off his hat and holds it twice before setting it down. His claim is a laugh, he says. Not the sound so much as the startle of it. The way the ribs lighten and the throat opens. He didn’t feel it at the time—he was reaching for his wallet, distracted by the steam from the soup, his wife’s hand on the table, and then it was gone, he says, and months later he went looking and never found it.

We have at least seven starts of a laugh in a Tupperware that smells faintly of oranges. I bring it to the counter and the lid wobbles. You can’t keep happiness sealed; it ferments, it becomes something else. He peels back the edge and breathes. His eyes cloud. I have to tell him it will not sound like her. He nods. He knew that when he gave me the number. He knows it now like an ache under the kneecap. He takes one start of a laugh between two fingers—it’s smaller than he expected, always—and presses it to his sternum as if returning a button. The bell on the desk rings as the heat shifts. Outside, a bus sighs. The man walks away a fraction of a rib lighter.

A girl comes with her mother and a sticky hand. She wants a shadow shaped like a dog. Hers keeps turning into a houseplant. Shadows are capricious but not cruel, so we go to the supply of soft hours and find one with low sun and knees in the dirt. The girl kneels and coaxes the shadow out with a whistle only she can hear. When the dog appears, the mother’s relief is a warm breeze over a bowl of soup you have just stopped blowing on. They leave holding hands, their shadows tangling on the worn linoleum.

At lunch, I eat toast over the sink. The building hums. Behind the cabinets, something rattles that refuses to be a mouse. We get deliveries by dumbwaiter from the post office floor. Dead letters are paperweight to what we handle.
